symptom: AGC WAF: JS Challenge action not working with Bot Manager Ruleset
rootCause: JS Challenge action is not supported in Application Gateway for Containers WAF during preview
solution: Set Bot Manager Ruleset actions to Block, Log, or Allow instead of JS Challenge
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Features/Feature WAF support in Application Gateway for containers

symptom: AGC WAF: Using unsupported OWASP ruleset (CRS 3.0/3.1/3.2) causes 500 error when associating WAF policy to AGC
rootCause: Only Default Ruleset (DRS) 2.1 is supported on AGC WAF; Core Ruleset (OWASP) 3.0, 3.1, 3.2 are not supported
solution: Use Default Ruleset (DRS) 2.1 as the managed ruleset instead of OWASP CRS 3.x
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Features/Feature WAF support in Application Gateway for containers

symptom: AGC WAF: Deleting AGC parent resource fails when child security policy resources exist
rootCause: Child security policy resources are auto-removed but deletion of parent may fail if references not cleaned up first
solution: Remove child resource security policy references from the security policy configuration before deleting the AGC parent resource
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Features/Feature WAF support in Application Gateway for containers

symptom: AGC WAF: Brief period of WAF policy not applied when moving policy target up the hierarchy
rootCause: When editing WAF policy to move target from lower level (Path) to higher level (Gateway), there is a transient gap in policy enforcement
solution: Be aware of brief gap during hierarchy change; plan WAF policy changes during maintenance windows if possible
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Features/Feature WAF support in Application Gateway for containers

symptom: AGC: Mixing BYO and ManagedBy Gateway YAML references breaks frontend - using URI reference (BYO) together with name/namespace reference (ManagedBy) in Gateway spec causes misconfiguration
rootCause: BYO AGC references the AGC resource URI in Gateway YAML, while ManagedBy references the ALB name and namespace. Mixing or using all three reference styles in the same Gateway causes the frontend to break.
solution: Ensure consistent Gateway YAML reference style: use only URI-based reference (infrastructureRef) for BYO deployments, or only name/namespace-based reference for ManagedBy deployments. Do not mix reference styles.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/How To/2- BYO and ManagedBy AGC - How to tell, why it matters, and how it affects WAF (Part 2 of video series)

symptom: AGC BYO: WAF WebApplicationFirewallPolicy YAML applied but WAF not enforcing rules - policy appears applied in kubectl but traffic is not being inspected
rootCause: In BYO AGC mode, applying a WebApplicationFirewallPolicy YAML does not auto-create the security policy object (unlike ManagedBy mode where it is auto-generated). Without the security policy association, WAF cannot enforce rules.
solution: For BYO AGC, manually create the security policy by associating the WAF policy to the AGC resource via Azure Portal, CLI, or PowerShell. This creates the necessary security policy linkage that ManagedBy mode handles automatically.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/How To/2- BYO and ManagedBy AGC - How to tell, why it matters, and how it affects WAF (Part 2 of video series)

symptom: AGC HTTPRoute cannot reference backend Service in a different AKS namespace - cross-namespace backend reference fails silently
rootCause: AKS namespaces are isolated by default and do not allow cross-namespace references for general objects. Without a ReferenceGrant, HTTPRoute in one namespace cannot reach a Service in another namespace.
solution: Create a ReferenceGrant resource in the destination namespace (where the backend Service lives) that explicitly allows the HTTPRoute from the source namespace to reference the Service. Example: kind: ReferenceGrant, from: HTTPRoute in source-namespace, to: Service in destination-namespace.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/How To/Cross-namespace references and how to allow them inside of AKS

symptom: AGC: Customer requests to enable FIPS on Application Gateway for Containers but no self-service option available
rootCause: FIPS requires a feature flag to be enabled at the subscription level by the product group; it is not enabled by default and cannot be self-service toggled
solution: Submit a Sev3 ICM to PG using ASC Application Gateway for Containers templates to request FIPS feature flag enablement on the subscription. Verify status using the Jarvis Action. Note: once enabled, all newly provisioned gateways will use FIPS and individual gateways cannot revert to non-FIPS mode.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/How To/How To Enable FIPS on Application Gateway for Containers

symptom: AGC: Customer wants to restrict/limit clients that can connect to AGC to only traffic from a specific Azure Front Door profile
rootCause: AGC does not natively support direct client IP restriction or access control lists
solution: Use Gateway API HTTPRoute conditional routing with X-Azure-FDID header matching. Configure HTTPRoute with a header match rule for X-Azure-FDID set to the Front Door profile ID, and do NOT define a default backendRef. Traffic not matching the header condition will receive a 404 response.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/How To/Limiting clients that can connect to AGC to a Front Door profile

symptom: AGC: Customer asks about connectivity and throughput limits for AGC that are not publicly documented
rootCause: Max numbers are subjective to different configurations and request characteristics (size, protocol, etc.) and are therefore not publicly documented
solution: Advise the customer to run their own performance/load tests using tools such as 'hey' (https://github.com/rakyll/hey) or 'vegeta' (https://github.com/tsenart/vegeta) against their specific gateway configuration
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/How To/Responding to requests about AGC limits that are not documented publicly

symptom: AGC: Azure Key Vault with Secrets Store CSI driver does not work for mounting SSL certificates into AGC - certificates cannot be loaded from AKV
rootCause: AGC requires certificates to be local to the AKS cluster as Kubernetes secrets. Certificates mounted into volumes via AKV Secrets Store CSI driver are not supported by AGC.
solution: Manually import SSL certificates into AKS using kubectl: 1) Extract private key from PFX with openssl pkcs12, 2) Decrypt the key with openssl rsa, 3) Extract public chain with openssl pkcs12 -clcerts -nokeys, 4) Create kubernetes TLS secret: kubectl create secret tls <name> -n <namespace> --cert public.crt --key decrypted.key. Place the secret in the same namespace as the ALB Controller.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/How To/Working with SSL certificates inside of AKS

symptom: Application Gateway returns 400 error when URL length exceeds 32KB limit
rootCause: Application Gateway has a hard limit of 32KB for maximum URL length and 32KB for maximum header size; exceeding this limit results in 400 Bad Request
solution: Customer must reduce URL length to stay within the 32KB limit. See Azure subscription service limits for Application Gateway.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/4xx response - Application Gateway URL too long

symptom: ApplicationGatewayAccessLogs show periodic requests with Nmap user-agent from AppGW private frontend IP or localhost (127.0.0.1)
rootCause: Known Azure Application Gateway platform bug (Bug 9729519): internal diagnostic loopback traffic is incorrectly surfaced in customer-facing access logs with Nmap user-agent
solution: No customer action required. This is expected internal platform diagnostic traffic. Platform fix targeted for Q2 CY2026.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Appgw logs showing Nmap User-Agent Traffic

symptom: Application Gateway CPU compute unit (CU) spikes even when customer traffic does not match the spike pattern
rootCause: Mandatory Qualys security scanning causes periodic CPU spikes, limited to max 5% CPU at lowest system priority
solution: Expected behavior. Qualys scans are mandatory for S360 compliance. CPU capped at 5%. Extra CUs count toward billing if exceeding reserved capacity. Do not raise CRI unless deviation from pattern.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/CPU Spike After Internal Qualys Security Scan

symptom: Application Gateway PUT operation fails with Backend Address Pool cannot have duplicate addresses when two backend pools reference same VMSS NIC/IP
rootCause: Known bug (Bug 9989909): validation does not prevent VMSS instance from having the same address in multiple backend address pools
solution: Remove duplicate IPs from both backend pools in a single PUT operation (one by one will fail). Use PowerShell: Get-AzApplicationGateway, filter BackendAddresses, Set-AzApplicationGateway, then PUT on VMSS.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Backend Address Pool cannot have duplicate addresses

symptom: ALB Controller fails to start or reports invalid configuration (error codes E1000-E1005): invalid command line arguments, invalid log level, Status Updater or Reconciler client failed to start
rootCause: Invalid ALB Controller startup parameters or internal component initialization failure
solution: Verify ALB Controller deployment arguments and log level settings (valid: panic/fatal/error/warn/info/debug/trace/disabled). For internal errors (E1002-E1005), check controller pod logs and redeploy if needed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Troubleshooting Guides/TSG: ALB Error Codes in Application Gateway for Containers

symptom: AGC configuration build errors (E2000-E2005): invalid default backend for Ingress, invalid rule path/type, invalid port, invalid session affinity type
rootCause: Misconfigured Ingress YAML - invalid backend service reference, unsupported rule path type, undefined port, or unsupported session affinity type
solution: Verify Ingress YAML: ensure default backend references a valid K8s Service, rules have valid paths with supported types, service ports are defined, and session affinity type is 'application-cookie' or 'managed-cookie'.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Troubleshooting Guides/TSG: ALB Error Codes in Application Gateway for Containers

symptom: AGC Kubernetes resource not found errors (E3000-E3017): missing endpoint slices, nodes, services, namespaces, pods, secrets, GatewayClass, IngressClass, BackendTLSPolicy, HealthCheckPolicy, RoutePolicy, ReferenceGrant, or HTTPRoutes
rootCause: Required Kubernetes resources referenced in AGC configuration do not exist on the cluster
solution: Create the missing Kubernetes resources as indicated in the error message. Common fixes: create endpoint slices for services, ensure at least one running node/pod, create required secrets, verify GatewayClass/IngressClass exist, create ReferenceGrants for cross-namespace references.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Troubleshooting Guides/TSG: ALB Error Codes in Application Gateway for Containers

symptom: AGC ARM CRUD operation failures (E4000-E4005): invalid ARM resource ID, failed to create/delete managed Frontend, failed to deploy AGC resource, missing URL for AGC resource, failed to update association
rootCause: Invalid ARM resource ID format or internal ARM CRUD operation failure in the AGC service
solution: For E4000: verify the ARM resource ID format is valid. For E4001-E4005 (internal errors): check ARM activity logs, verify RBAC permissions on the AGC resource, and escalate to PG if the issue persists.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Troubleshooting Guides/TSG: ALB Error Codes in Application Gateway for Containers

symptom: AGC gRPC/service connectivity failures (E5000-E5003): failed to connect via gRPC, internal errors, timeout waiting for deletion response, failed to receive backend health
rootCause: gRPC connectivity failure between ALB Controller and AGC service, or service-side timeout/internal error
solution: Check AGC service health and network connectivity. For E5000: verify gRPC endpoint is reachable. For E5001-E5003 (internal/timeout errors): check AGC resource status in Azure Portal, review ARM activity logs, and escalate to PG.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Troubleshooting Guides/TSG: ALB Error Codes in Application Gateway for Containers

symptom: AGC Gateway API YAML misconfiguration: gateway/httproute/backendtlspolicy/applicationloadbalancer shows errors in status section (e.g. 'Invalid hostname for listener')
rootCause: Invalid configuration in Gateway API YAML resources (e.g. invalid hostname format, conflicting listener config)
solution: Check status section of Kubernetes resources using 'kubectl get <resource> -A -o yaml' (gateway, httproute, backendtlspolicy, applicationloadbalancer). Fix the reported configuration errors in the YAML definitions.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Troubleshooting Guides/TSG: Configuration Troubleshooting flow for Application Gateway for Containers

symptom: AGC ALB Controller cannot deploy or manage routing rules due to workload identity misconfiguration
rootCause: Workload identity environment variables (AZURE_CLIENT_ID, AZURE_TENANT_ID, AZURE_FEDERATED_TOKEN_FILE) not set correctly on ALB Controller pod, or identity lacks required role assignments ('Azure Traffic Controller Configuration Manager')
solution: 1) Verify env vars on ALB controller pod: kubectl get pod -n azure-alb-system -l app=alb-controller -o jsonpath='{.items[0].spec.containers[0].env}'. 2) Check role assignments: for BYO — identity needs 'Azure Traffic Controller Configuration Manager' on AGC resource; for managed — identity needs 'Azure Traffic Controller Configuration Manager' + Contributor on MC resource group. 3) If roles are present, check ALB Controller and Control Plane logs.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Troubleshooting Guides/TSG: Configuration Troubleshooting flow for Application Gateway for Containers

symptom: AGC returns HTTP 500 due to invalid backend entries in HTTPRoute - backend references unknown/unsupported resource kind, non-existent resource, or cross-namespace resource without ReferenceGrant
rootCause: HTTPRoute backend references an invalid resource: unknown kind (InvalidKind), missing resource (BackendNotFound), or cross-namespace ref without ReferenceGrant (RefNotPermitted). If multiple backends with equal weights and one is invalid, 50% of traffic returns 500.
solution: Check HTTPRoute status conditions for InvalidKind/BackendNotFound/RefNotPermitted. Fix backend references: ensure correct resource kind, verify backend exists, or create ReferenceGrant for cross-namespace references.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Troubleshooting Guides/TSG: Data plane and Performance Troubleshooting flow for Application Gateway for Containers

symptom: AGC returns HTTP 500 because no endpoints found for all backends referenced in HTTPRoute
rootCause: All backends referenced in the HTTPRoute have zero endpoints (no running pods matching the backend service selectors)
solution: Verify backend pods are running and healthy with matching selectors. Check endpoint slices: kubectl get endpointslices -n <namespace>. Ensure pods have correct labels matching Service selectors.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Troubleshooting Guides/TSG: Data plane and Performance Troubleshooting flow for Application Gateway for Containers

symptom: AGC returns HTTP 503 - backend is not reachable or not healthy
rootCause: Backend is unreachable due to: (1) association subnet not in same VNet as AKS cluster, or (2) backend health probe failing
solution: 1. Verify association subnet is in same VNet as AKS cluster. 2. Check backend healthy targets via Traffic Stats dashboard (Jarvis). 3. Check backend health probe logging in Jarvis TrafficController/BackendServerDiagnosticHistory. 4. Validate health probe config via HealthCheckPolicy YAML or pod ReadinessProbe (path, port, protocol).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Troubleshooting Guides/TSG: Data plane and Performance Troubleshooting flow for Application Gateway for Containers

symptom: AGC returns HTTP 404 - no HTTPRoute matches the incoming request host/path/query combination
rootCause: No HTTPRoute is configured that matches the request's host, path, and query parameters, or the HTTPRoute has error conditions preventing it from being programmed
solution: 1. Verify an HTTPRoute exists matching the request host/path/query. 2. Check HTTPRoute status for Accepted and Ready conditions. 3. Fix any error conditions reported in HTTPRoute status. 4. Check ALB Controller logs for configuration errors.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Troubleshooting Guides/TSG: Data plane and Performance Troubleshooting flow for Application Gateway for Containers

symptom: AppGW + Key Vault: 'Data or Password for certificate is invalid' error after NRP successfully downloads cert from Key Vault but fails to install on Gateway Manager
rootCause: Certificate in Key Vault is corrupt or improperly bundled (wrong format, bad password, incorrect cert chain order)
solution: Customer needs to re-bundle the certificate correctly and re-upload it to Key Vault. Azure Networking cannot assist with certificate formatting — only communication and connectivity troubleshooting.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Configure and Troubleshoot Application Gateway + Key Vault Issues

symptom: AppGW + Key Vault: HTTPS listener disabled after customer deletes SSL certificate from Key Vault. Error: 'KeyVault Secret not found'. AppGW does NOT go into Failed state.
rootCause: Customer deleted the SSL certificate/secret from Key Vault, but AppGW still references it. AppGW disables the affected HTTPS listener as expected behavior.
solution: Re-upload the SSL certificate to Key Vault, then go to the Listener blade and re-select the new certificate: choose Managed Identity → Key Vault → new certificate.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Configure and Troubleshoot Application Gateway + Key Vault Issues

symptom: AppGW + Key Vault: ClientAssertionCredential authentication failed — 'The certificate with identifier used to sign the client assertion is not registered on application' (AADSTS700027)
rootCause: Known issue: Managed Identity certificate registration becomes stale or corrupted on the application side.
solution:
Reapply the Managed Identity on AppGW via PowerShell:
$AppGw = Get-AzApplicationGateway -Name $AppGwName -ResourceGroupName $RGName
$identity = Get-AzUserAssignedIdentity -Name $UserIdentityName -ResourceGroupName $rgname
Set-AzApplicationGatewayIdentity -ApplicationGateway $AppGw -UserAssignedIdentityId $identity.Id
Set-AzApplicationGateway -ApplicationGateway $AppGw
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Configure and Troubleshoot Application Gateway + Key Vault Issues

symptom: AppGW in Failed state after customer deleted certificate from Key Vault but did NOT remove the SSL certificate reference from Application Gateway
rootCause: Orphaned/unused SSL certificate reference in AppGW configuration still points to deleted Key Vault secret, causing validation failure on every PUT operation.
solution:
Remove the unused SSL certificate from AppGW via PowerShell:
$AppGW = Get-AzApplicationGateway -Name 'AppGwName' -ResourceGroupName 'RGName'
Remove-AzApplicationGatewaySslCertificate -ApplicationGateway $AppGW -Name 'CertificateName'
Set-AzApplicationGateway -ApplicationGateway $AppGw
Or via CLI: az network application-gateway ssl-cert delete -g RGName --gateway-name AppGwName -n CertificateName
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Configure and Troubleshoot Application Gateway + Key Vault Issues

symptom: AppGW + Key Vault: 'Key Vault doesn't allow access to the managed identity' error when configuring certificate in Listener blade
rootCause: Customer selected 'Create New' instead of 'Select Existing' when configuring the certificate in the Listener blade after already setting up Managed Identity and Key Vault access.
solution: Ensure the option 'Select Existing' is selected in the Listener blade when configuring the certificate, not 'Create New'. The managed identity and Key Vault access must already be configured.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Configure and Troubleshoot Application Gateway + Key Vault Issues

symptom: AppGW V2 billing overcharge: customer reports higher-than-expected charges for autoscaling Application Gateway v2. Portal creates autoscaling GW with minimum 2 instances (cannot set 0 or 1 in portal).
rootCause: Azure Portal bug: when creating an autoscaling Application Gateway v2, the portal forces a minimum of 2 instances. Each instance is billed for 10 Capacity Units regardless of traffic. Customer is overcharged for the extra minimum instance.
solution:
After creation, use PowerShell to decrease MinCapacity:
$appgw = Get-AzApplicationGateway -ResourceGroupName $RG -Name $AppGWNAME
$appgw = Set-AzApplicationGatewayAutoscaleConfiguration -ApplicationGateway $appgw -MinCapacity 0 -MaxCapacity 2
Set-AzApplicationGateway -ApplicationGateway $appgw
Explain workaround to customer and suggest billing engineer offer a refund.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/AppGW V2 Billing Clarification

symptom: Authorization header is stripped/null after Application Gateway redirect to external site
rootCause: HTTP clients strip the Authorization header after a redirect for security purposes, especially when redirected to a different domain/origin. This is standard HTTP behavior documented in .NET HttpWebRequest and Chrome.
solution: Use Application Gateway rewrite rules instead of redirect rules to an external site. Rewrite rules preserve headers including Authorization.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/Authorization Header being stripped followed by a redirect

symptom: Connection drops and HTTP 502/504 errors during Application Gateway instance version upgrade (automatic OS upgrade). WebSocket sessions terminate intermittently.
rootCause: During rolling OS upgrade, instance count temporarily exceeds configured threshold (e.g., 50→53). When backend pool crosses 50 instances, Azure LB SNAT port allocation drops from 1024 to 512 per instance, causing SNAT port reallocation that tears down existing outbound connections. Oscillation around the threshold causes repeated port reassignments.
solution: Use Azure NAT Gateway for outbound traffic to avoid SNAT port exhaustion and dynamic reallocation. NAT Gateway provides up to 512K ports and decouples port allocation from VM count. Check: 1) AppGW Platform dashboard for instance count fluctuation 2) Shoebox logs for failed request spike alignment 3) SNAT Dashboard for port allocation changes 4) ASC Operations for 'Invoking action VirtualMachineScaleSets.AutoOSUpgrade.POST'
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/Connection Drops due to AppGW Instances Upgrade

symptom: Application Gateway goes to Failed state with InternalServerError. GWM logs show MaxUnhealthyUpgradedInstancePercentExceededInRollingUpgrade. Tenant logs show 'Client address is not authorized' when accessing KeyVault secret.
rootCause: KeyVault firewall does not allow AppGW subnet. When Service Endpoint for KeyVault is enabled on AppGW subnet, the KeyVault firewall must explicitly allow that subnet. Misconfigured virtual network rules cause AppGW instances to fail certificate retrieval.
solution: Three options: 1) Allow AppGW subnet in KeyVault firewall virtual network rules 2) Enable 'Allow trusted Microsoft services' on KeyVault firewall 3) Allow AppGW Public IP on KeyVault firewall. Verify: AppGW subnet has KeyVault Service Endpoint enabled AND KeyVault firewall has the correct AppGW subnet allowed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/Operation failures on Application Gateways

symptom: AppGW goes down — CPU, memory utilization, and instance count all drop to zero. GWM shows MaxUnhealthyUpgradedInstancePercentExceededInRollingUpgrade with connection timeouts during CustomScriptExtensions.
rootCause: A default route (0.0.0.0/0) was propagated to the AppGW subnet via ExpressRoute or VPN gateway peering, breaking control plane connectivity. This is a documented unsupported scenario.
solution: 1) Create a UDR on AppGW subnet to disable BGP route propagation, OR 2) Create a UDR with 0.0.0.0/0 pointing to Internet next hop. Verify: Check peered VNet for 'useRemoteGateways=true', then query GatewayTenantLogsTable for 'Updated adjacency table' messages containing '0.0.0.0'. Ref: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/application-gateway/configuration-infrastructure#v2-supported-scenarios
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/Operation failures on Application Gateways

symptom: Application Gateway returns HTTP 408 responses under load or when there are large amounts of TCP retransmits. Access logs show no host header.
rootCause: Throughput issue on the data path between client and gateway. The 408 occurs when AppGW receives a TCP connection but never receives a Layer 7 (HTTP) request within the timeout period.
solution: 1) Review datapath performance and throughput for possible packet drops 2) Review client-side captures to verify the client is actually sending the HTTP request after TCP handshake. Note: This is a rare edge case — AppGW does not normally present 408s.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting 408s presented from appgw

symptom: PUT operations on Application Gateway fail with error: 'Data or KeyVaultSecretId must be specified for certificate'. Subsequent operations also fail.
rootCause: SSL certificate on Application Gateway has no PublicCertData and no KeyVaultSecretId — the certificate reference is stuck/orphaned (both fields show N/A in ASC Properties).
solution: Solution 1: In Azure Portal → AppGW → Listeners → Listener TLS certificates, find the affected cert and re-upload the .pfx file or link to a KeyVault certificate. Solution 2: Use PowerShell to create a new SSL certificate (New-AzApplicationGatewaySslCertificate), update the listener to use the new cert (Set-AzApplicationGatewayHttpListener), remove the old cert (Remove-AzApplicationGatewaySslCertificate), then apply (Set-AzApplicationGateway). Before troubleshooting, verify: 1) AppGW has connectivity to KeyVault 2) Referenced secret/certificate is not disabled.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Data or KeyVaultSecretId must be specified

symptom: Application Gateway redirects client to backend default FQDN (e.g. azurewebsites.net) or backend IP instead of the frontend domain configured on AppGW. Occurs when hostname override is enabled in backend settings.
rootCause: When 'Pick hostname from backend pool' or hostname override is configured in backend HTTP settings, AppGW rewrites the Host header to the backend's default domain. Backend applications building absolute URL redirects (Location header) use this overwritten hostname, causing the client browser to connect directly to the backend instead of through AppGW.
solution: Three options: (1) Add a custom domain to the backend app service matching the AppGW frontend domain, then configure hostname override to use that custom domain or remove hostname override entirely. (2) Change the application code to use relative URL redirects instead of absolute URL redirects. (3) Use AppGW header rewrite rules to rewrite the Location header in responses.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/App Gateway Redirects To Backend FQDN or IP

symptom: Application Gateway v2 backend health shows 'Unknown' status intermittently. Multiple DNS resolver servers configured on the VNET. Some backends alternate between healthy and unknown.
rootCause: AppGW does not follow standard Azure DNS resolver ordering (primary/secondary). On startup or PUT, it tries each DNS resolver and latches onto the first one that responds. If that resolver cannot resolve backend hostnames or AppGW dependency hostnames, backend health reports as Unknown. With 2 out of 4 DNS resolvers unable to resolve, the issue is ephemeral.
solution: Ensure ALL DNS resolver servers configured on the AppGW VNET can resolve both public hostnames (required for AppGW dependencies) and backend hostnames. Use the DNS verification tool to confirm resolution from AppGW instances.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/Application Gateway instances configured with multiple DNS resolver servers causing issues

symptom: Application Gateway health probes fail only from specific AppGW instances while other instances report the same backend as healthy. Affected instances return 502 errors. Replacing the faulty instance temporarily resolves but the issue recurs on a new instance.
rootCause: Backend server has IP-based blocking (e.g., Fail2Ban, iptables, custom firewall rules) that blocks traffic from specific AppGW instance IPs. Backend OS responds with ICMP Port Unreachable to SYN packets from blocked IPs. Other causes include NSG/UDR misconfiguration or rate-limiting on the backend.
solution: Check backend server firewall rules with 'iptables -L -n -v'. Look for blocked AppGW instance IPs (e.g., Fail2Ban rules). Customer must allow connections from all AppGW instance IPs. Also check NSG rules and UDR configurations for inconsistencies. Take packet captures on backend filtering by AppGW instance IP to confirm SYN/ICMP behavior.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/Backend Blocking Traffic from Specific AppGW Instances

symptom: Application Gateway resolves privatelink/private zone FQDNs (e.g., something.privatelink.web.core.windows.net) via public endpoint instead of custom DNS, even though custom DNS servers with proper private zone linking are configured on the VNET.
rootCause: AppGW has hard-coded DNS exceptions — domains matching .windows.net, .azure.net, .microsoft.com and others are always resolved via Azure DNS (168.63.129.16) regardless of custom DNS configuration. This is by design to ensure AppGW can reach its internal dependencies. The exception is implemented in dnsmasq configuration (appgwdnsmasq.conf).
solution: Two workarounds: (1) Add the private DNS zone directly to the VNET where AppGW is deployed (instead of relying on forwarding through custom DNS in another VNET). (2) Use the backend IP address instead of FQDN in the backend pool, but set the correct hostname in backend settings and health probe. Note: If EnableApplicationGatewayNetworkIsolation feature is registered, the DNS exception is disabled in the customer namespace.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/Custom DNS Internal Exceptions

symptom: Azure CLI command 'az network application-gateway address-pool update --remove backendIpConfigurations' fails to remove a NIC/server from the Application Gateway backend pool.
rootCause: AppGW does not autonomously update IP values when referencing VMs. It relies on the VM NIC to manage and update IP values. The command targeting AppGW directly fails because the association is managed from the NIC side, not the AppGW side.
solution: Use 'az network nic ip-config address-pool remove' instead of 'az network application-gateway address-pool update --remove'. Specify the full address-pool resource ID, the NIC IP config name, the NIC name, and the resource group of the NIC. Example: az network nic ip-config address-pool remove --address-pool "/subscriptions/.../backendAddressPools/pool-name" -n "NIC-IP-CONFIG-NAME" --nic-name "NIC-NAME" --resource-group "NIC-RG"
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/Removing Server from Backend Pool using Azure CLI

symptom: VAPT (Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing) reports flag Application Gateway affinity cookie as a security vulnerability because the Set-Cookie header lacks HTTPOnly and Secure attributes.
rootCause: The AppGW affinity cookie is a one-way hash of the backend pool VM's IP address used for session affinity. It does not contain customer data, login credentials, or session information — those are in cookies set by the backend server. The VAPT finding is a false alarm because even if the hash were cracked, it would only expose the backend server's IP/FQDN, not sensitive data.
solution: Explain to the customer that this is a false alarm. The AppGW affinity cookie is a one-way hash that does not contain sensitive information. Even if reversed, it only reveals the backend IP/FQDN, not customer data. HTTPOnly and Secure flags on this cookie are on the AppGW roadmap for future enhancement but the current behavior does not pose a real security risk.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Application Gateway Affinity ,Cookie Handling, Vulnerabilities, and False Alarms

symptom: Log Analytics Workspace shows error: 'Data of type AzureDiagnostics is being dropped due to incorrect format at lineOffset. Exception message: After parsing a value an unexpected character was encountered: B.' AppGW access logs are partially lost.
rootCause: Invalid UTF-8 data in client request fields (requestUri, UserAgent, contentType). When these fields contain invalid UTF-8 characters, the Geneva agent replaces them with placeholder values like '[789 B blob data]', which breaks JSON parsing and causes the entire log batch to be dropped.
solution: Known issue with repair item 15608290. Fix rolled out for requestUri and User-Agent fields. Tenant release 30.0 extends fix to originalRequestUriWithArgs, requestQuery, contentType, and originalHost. To identify affected records, query _LogOperation for 'Data of type AzureDiagnostics is being dropped' errors. For deeper debugging, JIT into the AppGW instance and check /appgwroot/log/nginx/access.log for the raw log entries.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Data of type AzureDiagnostics is being dropped due to incorrect format

symptom: Application Gateway timing metrics (e.g., ApplicationGatewayTotalTime, Backend Last Byte Response Time) show an upward latency trend immediately after instance restart or worker process recycling, but actual request-response logs show no change in latency.
rootCause: Inactive listeners (not receiving traffic) continue to report 0-value data points in metrics until instance restart. After restart, the metrics pipeline resets and these 0-value data points disappear, causing the average to increase artificially. The request-response logs don't include inactive listeners, so they show the true latency.
solution: Always split timing metrics by listener to avoid the averaging issue with inactive listeners. Verify with AzureMetrics query: AzureMetrics | where MetricName == 'ApplicationGatewayTotalTime' | summarize avg(Count) by TimeGenerated — a drop in data point count correlating with latency increase confirms this is the cause. Remove or reconfigure unused listeners to prevent recurrence.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Expert Troubleshooting/Appgw Timings discrepancies in metrics

symptom: AppGW+KeyVault: Data or Password for certificate is invalid after NRP downloads cert from KV but fails to install on GWM
rootCause: Certificate in Key Vault is corrupt or improperly bundled (wrong format, bad password, incorrect cert chain order)
solution: Customer must re-bundle the certificate correctly and re-upload to Key Vault. Azure Networking only handles communication/connectivity troubleshooting.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Configure and Troubleshoot Application Gateway + Key Vault Issues

symptom: AppGW+KeyVault: HTTPS listener disabled after customer deletes SSL cert from KV. Error: KeyVault Secret not found. AppGW does NOT enter Failed state.
rootCause: Customer deleted SSL certificate/secret from Key Vault but AppGW still references it. AppGW disables the affected HTTPS listener.
solution: Re-upload SSL certificate to Key Vault, then re-select in Listener blade: choose Managed Identity -> Key Vault -> new certificate.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Configure and Troubleshoot Application Gateway + Key Vault Issues

symptom: AppGW+KeyVault: ClientAssertionCredential authentication failed - AADSTS700027 certificate used to sign client assertion is not registered on application
rootCause: Known issue: Managed Identity certificate registration becomes stale/corrupted on the application side.
solution: Reapply Managed Identity on AppGW via PowerShell: Get-AzApplicationGateway -> Get-AzUserAssignedIdentity -> Set-AzApplicationGatewayIdentity -> Set-AzApplicationGateway
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Configure and Troubleshoot Application Gateway + Key Vault Issues

symptom: AppGW in Failed state after customer deleted certificate from Key Vault but did NOT remove SSL cert reference from Application Gateway
rootCause: Orphaned/unused SSL certificate reference in AppGW config still points to deleted KV secret, causing validation failure on every PUT operation.
solution: Remove unused SSL cert via PS: Remove-AzApplicationGatewaySslCertificate -ApplicationGateway $AppGW -Name CertName; Set-AzApplicationGateway. Or CLI: az network application-gateway ssl-cert delete
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Configure and Troubleshoot Application Gateway + Key Vault Issues

symptom: AppGW+KeyVault: Key Vault does not allow access to the managed identity error when configuring certificate in Listener blade
rootCause: Customer selected Create New instead of Select Existing when configuring certificate in Listener blade after already setting up Managed Identity and KV access.
solution: Use Select Existing in the Listener blade when configuring certificate, not Create New. Managed identity and Key Vault access must already be configured.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Configure and Troubleshoot Application Gateway + Key Vault Issues

symptom: AppGW V2 billing overcharge: portal creates autoscaling GW with minimum 2 instances (cannot set 0/1 in portal). 10 CUs per instance billed regardless of traffic.
rootCause: Azure Portal bug forces minimum 2 instances for autoscaling AppGW v2. Customer overcharged for extra minimum instance capacity.
solution: After creation, use PS: Set-AzApplicationGatewayAutoscaleConfiguration -ApplicationGateway $appgw -MinCapacity 0 -MaxCapacity 2. Explain workaround and suggest billing refund.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/AppGW V2 Billing Clarification

symptom: When Application Gateway overrides backend hostname, the Set-Cookie ARRAffinity header Domain attribute contains the backend FQDN instead of the original client-facing domain. Browsers reject the cookie due to domain mismatch, causing session management failures, login issues, and pages continuously rendering.
rootCause: Azure App Service sets the Set-Cookie Domain attribute based on the incoming Host header. When Application Gateway overrides the hostname to the backend FQDN (e.g., non-existing-app.azurewebsites.net), the cookie domain doesn't match the client's original hostname (e.g., test-gateway.test).
solution: Set clientAffinityProxyEnabled=true on the App Service: az resource update --name <app-name> --resource-type 'Microsoft.Web/sites' -g <rg> --set properties.clientAffinityProxyEnabled=true. This makes the web app inspect X-Original-Host or X-Forwarded-Host headers from Application Gateway/Front Door/CDN and dynamically set the Set-Cookie Domain attribute to match the original client hostname.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Azure App Service Session Affinity Cookie

symptom: When accessing Application Gateway using host:port format (e.g., https://test-gateway.test:4443), the Set-Cookie ARRAffinity Domain attribute includes the port number (Domain=test-gateway.test:4443). Cookie specifications disallow ports in Domain, so browsers and applications reject the cookie, breaking session management.
rootCause: Application Gateway's X-Original-Host header is derived from server variable var_http_host which includes the port. When clientAffinityProxyEnabled is true, the App Service uses this value (including port) as the Set-Cookie Domain attribute, violating cookie specifications.
solution: Add a rewrite rule on Application Gateway to set the X-Forwarded-Host header for every request to the backend web app. Use the server variable var_host (hostname only, without port) as the value. The App Service will use X-Forwarded-Host instead of X-Original-Host, producing a valid cookie Domain without port.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Azure App Service Session Affinity Cookie

symptom: In Private-Only Application Gateway v2 deployments, backend health shows 'unknown' status with an error message incorrectly stating that NSG rules are required and route tables should be eliminated.
rootCause: Bug in Private-Only Gateway error message template: when backend health is unknown due to DNS resolution or other reasons, the error message still references legacy public IP requirements (NSG rules, UDR elimination) which don't apply to private-only deployments.
solution: The erroneous NSG/UDR error message can be safely ignored for Private-Only Gateway deployments. Investigate the actual underlying cause (typically DNS resolution). This error message typo will be fixed in a future release.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: Private-Only Gateway for Application Gateway v2

symptom: MySQL Flexible Server connections through Application Gateway Layer 4 TLS proxy fail with error: 'Lost connection to MySQL server at reading initial communication packet, system error: 0'. ReqResp logs show HTTP 500, certificate errors in error logs, and TLS handshake failures on MySQL side.
rootCause: MySQL protocol requires an initial non-SSL handshake exchange (server greeting + client capabilities) before the TLS handshake begins. When AppGW L4 is configured to terminate TLS, it expects a standard TLS Client Hello immediately, while MySQL sends its protocol-specific initial handshake packet first. Both sides wait for incompatible messages, causing timeout.
solution: Do not configure TLS/mTLS termination on Application Gateway L4 for MySQL Flexible Server backends. Use TCP mode instead (no SSL on AppGW) and let the MySQL server handle the SSL/TLS handshake directly end-to-end with the client.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/TSG:  Layer 4 Application Gateway TLS Proxy Limitations for MySQL Flexible Server

symptom: Application Gateway HTTPS listener returns wrong certificate (different CN name) when accessed, even though correct certificate was uploaded
rootCause: Listener does not have any routing rule associated. Without a rule, the listener is disabled in data-plane configuration. AppGW returns HTTP 404 and serves the certificate from the next working listener instead.
solution: Associate a routing rule with the listener before testing frontend HTTPS connectivity. A listener without a rule is not active in the data plane.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Application Gateway Listener Without Rule Associated

symptom: Application Gateway returns HTTP 403 for HTTP/2 traffic over cleartext (h2c)
rootCause: Azure Application Gateway only supports HTTP/2 for HTTPS listeners. HTTP listeners do not support HTTP/2 (h2c - HTTP/2 over cleartext).
solution: Use HTTPS listener for HTTP/2 support. Most browsers only support HTTP/2 over HTTPS anyway.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: HTTP2 Support

symptom: Data path failure after configuring public and private listeners on same port for Application Gateway v2
rootCause: AppGW version is below Vmss_12.0.1496.2. The same-port feature for public/private IPs requires at least this version. Older versions accept the control plane configuration but the data path is inoperative.
solution: Upgrade Application Gateway to the latest version (at least Vmss_12.0.1496.2). Post to [L7] Application Gateway Teams channel for TA review if needed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: Public and Private IP in the same port for Application Gateway v2

symptom: Traffic blocked after configuring same port for public/private IPs on Application Gateway v2 with NSG
rootCause: When same port is configured for public and private IPs, AppGW internally changes the destination for ALL inbound flows to the frontend IPs. NSG rules not updated to include both public and private frontend IPs as destination.
solution: Update NSG inbound rules: set Destination to both public and private frontend IPs of Application Gateway. Note: this affects ALL inbound flows, including other public listeners on different ports.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: Public and Private IP in the same port for Application Gateway v2

symptom: Cannot use both TLS 1.3 (Customv2) and legacy TLS policies (Custom) simultaneously on Application Gateway
rootCause: TLS 1.3 uses OpenSSL v1.1.1 (Customv2 policies) while older policies use OpenSSL v1.0.2 (Custom policies). Side-by-side installs of OpenSSL are not supported.
solution: Migrate fully to Customv2 policies for TLS 1.3 support. Cannot mix Custom and Customv2 policy types on the same Application Gateway.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: TLS 1.3 Support

symptom: Backend connections not using TLS 1.3 on Application Gateway even though TLS 1.3 is configured on frontend
rootCause: By design, backend connections only support TLS 1.0-1.2. TLS 1.3 is supported only for frontend/listener connections (client to AppGW).
solution: This is expected behavior. TLS 1.3 applies to frontend connections only. Backend server connections use TLS 1.0 minimum and up to TLS 1.2.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: TLS 1.3 Support

symptom: No data found in Log Analytics after configuring diagnostic settings for Application Gateway (Data not found in the last 48 hours)
rootCause: Destination table defaults to 'Resource-specific' instead of 'Azure Diagnostics'. Queries using AzureDiagnostics table (including suggested queries from portal) return no results because data goes to resource-specific tables.
solution: Either: (1) Change destination to 'Azure Diagnostics' in diagnostic settings and wait for new logs, or (2) Keep 'Resource-specific' and query using AGWAccessLogs, AGWFirewallLogs, AGWPerformanceLogs tables instead of AzureDiagnostics.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Log analytics workspace default configuration

symptom: Updating IDLE timeout for Public IP of a CSES-migrated Application Gateway V1 fails with error: PublicIPAddressUsedByCloudServiceCannotBeUpdated
rootCause: After CSES migration, Allocation Type and Idle Timeout settings of public IP addresses used by Cloud Services (Extended Support) cannot be changed via standard operations
solution: PG must disable CSES creation at subscription level (Feature: DisableAppgwCsesDeployments). Reach TA via Teams [L7]Application Gateway channel for CRI approval, then escalate using ICM template [AppGWv1 CSES Migration] Disable CSES creation for subscription (id:B231l1)
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Application Gateway Internal CSES Migration

symptom: Registering AllowApplicationGatewayLoadDistributionPolicy feature flag stays in Pending state indefinitely
rootCause: The Load Distribution feature for Azure Application Gateway has been cancelled/postponed with no ETA for public availability.
solution: Inform customer the feature is not available and has no ETA. Advise to reach out to their account team for roadmap info. Reference IcM 528953655.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: AllowApplicationGatewayLoadDistributionPolicy

symptom: Application Gateway WAF returns HTTP 500 error instead of expected custom block response. Nginx errors appear in AppGW logs.
rootCause: Multiple possible causes: (1) headers already transmitted, (2) failed to read WAF custom block response body file, (3) failed to allocate file buffer, (4) failed to send HTTP headers
solution: These are nginx errors indicating unexpected internal issues. Escalate to PG. Verify CRS ruleset >= 3.2 and API version >= 2022-07-01.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: Custom response for Azure Web Application Firewall

symptom: Mutual Authentication configuration fails with ApplicationGatewayTrustedClientCertificateDoesNotContainAnyCACertificate error
rootCause: Customer uploaded only a leaf certificate without a CA certificate. CA cert must have BasicConstraints (OID 2.5.29.19) set to CA:TRUE.
solution: Upload certificate chain including at least one CA cert (root cert). Leaf cert alone is not sufficient.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: Mutual Authentication

symptom: Access logs show sslClientVerify FAILED:unable to verify the first certificate with HTTP 403 on mutual auth endpoint
rootCause: Client presents only the leaf certificate whose issuer is not trusted. Trusted certificate chain is incomplete.
solution: Ensure complete trusted certificate chain (root + all intermediates) is uploaded to SSL Profile. Request client-side packet capture to verify TLS handshake.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: Mutual Authentication

symptom: Mutual auth fails with Unable to verify the client certificate issuer when VerifyClientCertIssuerDN is enabled
rootCause: Issuer DN of client cert does not match any ClientCertificateIssuerDN extracted from uploaded trusted chains. Matching depends on chain depth.
solution: Check ASC ValidatedCertData and ClientCertificateIssuerDN. For root->int->leaf: intermediate subject verified. For root->leaf: root subject. Multiple chains of different length: longer chain used.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: Mutual Authentication

symptom: Mutual authentication fails because client certificate has expired
rootCause: Client certificate in SSL Profile has expired and needs renewal.
solution: Portal: Open SSL Profile -> Upload renewed cert -> Remove old cert -> Save. PowerShell: Get-AzApplicationGateway -> Remove-AzApplicationGatewayTrustedClientCertificate -> Add new -> Set-AzApplicationGateway
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: Mutual Authentication

symptom: sslClientVerify shows NONE in access logs when accessing mutual auth endpoint. Client connection not authenticated.
rootCause: Client does not send client certificate. Client-side not configured correctly to use client certs.
solution: Check client-side cert setup: (1) verify certs installed on client, (2) test with OpenSSL, (3) verify browser cert selection. If strictEnforce=false, connection passes with empty auth status.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: Mutual Authentication

symptom: WebSocket session times out unexpectedly on Application Gateway V2 even though connection was established
rootCause: HTTP Settings request timeout also applies to WebSocket sessions. V2 terminates session if no Keep-Alive from client or no PING-PONG from backend.
solution: Configure PING-PONG intervals on backend application lower than AppGW HTTP Settings timeout value.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: Websockets Overview

symptom: No data found in Log Analytics after configuring diagnostic settings for Application Gateway
rootCause: Destination table defaults to Resource-specific instead of Azure Diagnostics. Queries using AzureDiagnostics table return no results.
solution: Either: (1) Change destination to Azure Diagnostics, or (2) Query using AGWAccessLogs, AGWFirewallLogs, AGWPerformanceLogs instead of AzureDiagnostics.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Log analytics workspace default configuration

symptom: When accessing Application Gateway using host:port format, the Set-Cookie ARRAffinity Domain includes the port number. Cookie specs disallow ports in Domain, so browsers reject the cookie.
rootCause: Application Gateway X-Original-Host header is derived from server variable var_http_host which includes the port. The App Service uses this value (with port) as Set-Cookie Domain, violating cookie specs.
solution: Add a rewrite rule on Application Gateway to set X-Forwarded-Host header using server variable var_host (hostname only, no port). The App Service will use X-Forwarded-Host instead of X-Original-Host.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Azure App Service Session Affinity Cookie

symptom: In Private-Only Application Gateway v2 deployments, backend health shows unknown status with error message incorrectly stating NSG rules are required and route tables should be eliminated.
rootCause: Bug in Private-Only Gateway error message template: when backend health is unknown due to DNS resolution or other reasons, the message still references legacy public IP requirements which do not apply.
solution: The erroneous NSG/UDR error message can be safely ignored for Private-Only Gateway. Investigate the actual underlying cause (typically DNS resolution). Fix planned for future release.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Features and Functions/Feature: Private-Only Gateway for Application Gateway v2

symptom: MySQL Flexible Server connections through Application Gateway Layer 4 TLS proxy fail with: Lost connection to MySQL server at reading initial communication packet, system error: 0. ReqResp logs show 500, certificate errors, and handshake failures.
rootCause: MySQL protocol requires initial non-SSL handshake (server greeting + client capabilities) before TLS. AppGW L4 TLS expects Client Hello immediately, while MySQL sends its handshake first. Both sides wait for incompatible messages, causing timeout.
solution: Do not configure TLS/mTLS termination on Application Gateway L4 for MySQL backends. Use TCP mode and let MySQL handle SSL directly end-to-end with the client.

symptom: Application Gateway session affinity not maintained; same session appears on multiple backend servers
rootCause: Cookie-based affinity not enabled in backendHttpSettingsCollection (cookieBasedAffinity set to Disabled)
solution: Enable Cookie Based Affinity in the HTTP Settings tab in Azure Portal or set cookieBasedAffinity to Enabled in ARM config
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Session Affinity Not Working

symptom: Application cannot use cookie-based session affinity on Application Gateway
rootCause: Customer application does not support cookie-based affinity mechanism
solution: Use Azure Load Balancer (ILB/SLB) with 5-tuple, 2-tuple, or 3-tuple distribution mode instead. Note: URL path rules and SSL offloading will not be available with Load Balancer
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Session Affinity Not Working

symptom: Requests bouncing between backend servers despite cookie-based affinity enabled; ARRAffinity cookie not retained by browser
rootCause: Client accessing Application Gateway via shortname URL (e.g. http://Website instead of FQDN). IE browser will not store or use cookies with shortname URLs
solution: Access website using FQDN (e.g. http://website.com). If FQDN cannot be used, switch to a layer-4 Load Balancer. Verify with Fiddler trace that client sends ARRAffinity cookie back
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Session Affinity Not Working

symptom: Cannot view zones in Azure Portal after migrating Application Gateway from non-zonal to zonal; zones field appears empty
rootCause: Migration from non-zonal to zonal runs silently; zones are not displayed in portal after migration to avoid further issues
solution: Navigate to ASC > download Raw Gateway Manager Config (SAS URL), search for PhysicalZones field to confirm the Application Gateway is zonal
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/TSG:  Unable to view zones after migrating from non-zonal

symptom: HTTP 400 Bad Request errors on Application Gateway V1; errors not visible in ReqRespLog
rootCause: Request is invalid at HTTP.sys kernel level (malformed headers, invalid framing) and rejected before reaching user mode/ARR
solution: Collect HTTPERR logs from %windir%/System32/logfiles/HTTPERR/HTTPERR#.LOG on AppGW V1 instances via TA. Use Log Parser to filter for status 400. For V2, check ReqRespLog only
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Troubleshoot 4XX Errors - Application Gateway

symptom: HTTP 400 errors on Application Gateway due to request URL or query string being too long
rootCause: Request URL or query string exceeds Application Gateway limits: maxUrl=8192, maxQueryString=8192
solution: Check request filtering settings limits. Current defaults: maxAllowedContentLength=2147483647, maxUrl=8192, maxQueryString=8192. Advise customer to ensure requests stay within limits
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Troubleshoot 4XX Errors - Application Gateway

symptom: Application Gateway diagnostic logs not showing in Azure Portal, Log Analytics, or PowerShell despite logging enabled
rootCause: AppGW cannot resolve FQDN of Storage Account for log storage, typically due to custom DNS. UDR or NSG may also block connectivity
solution: 1) Verify custom DNS IP in VNet. 2) Check no UDR/NSG blocking UDP 53 via TestTraffic in ASC. 3) Verify DNS resolution for storage FQDN and prod.warmpath.msftcloudes.com. 4) Check diagnostic settings. 5) Check storage firewall
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Troubleshoot Application Gateway Missing Logs

symptom: Application Gateway unable to write diagnostic logs to storage account
rootCause: Cipher suites misconfiguration affecting AppGW ability to connect to storage account over TLS
solution: Configure proper TLS cipher suites per Microsoft docs. At minimum one required cipher suite must be present
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Troubleshoot Application Gateway Missing Logs

symptom: 502 Bad Gateway or backend unhealthy when integrating APIM with Application Gateway
rootCause: Certificate thumbprint mismatch between AppGW and APIM, or default listeners/HTTP settings/rules not removed
solution: 1) Verify cert thumbprints match. 2) Remove default listeners, HTTP settings, and rule. 3) Test from VM in same VNET with hosts file. 4) If still failing, engage APIM team
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting API Management APIM

symptom: 503 Service Unavailable when accessing APIM through Application Gateway
rootCause: Default listener still present in Application Gateway configuration
solution: Remove default listener, default backend HTTP settings, and default rule from AppGW. If issue persists, engage APIM team
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting API Management APIM

symptom: HTTP 5xx errors on Application Gateway; need to determine origin (AppGW vs backend)
rootCause: Backend server returning 5xx which AppGW proxies. If backend returns 200 but client sees 5xx, indicates ARR module incompatibility
solution: Check ReqRespLog for SERVER-STATUS field. If SERVER-STATUS=5xx, issue is backend. If SERVER-STATUS=200 but httpStatus=5xx, investigate ARR/backend incompatibility
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting Application Gateway HTTP 5xx Errors

symptom: 503 from APIM behind AppGW when client uses hostname different from configured FQDN
rootCause: APIM returns 503 when request hostname does not match configured FQDN. Missing CNAME mapping
solution: Create CNAME mapping of APIM hostname to AppGW frontend DNS name. For private IP, map CNAME in custom DNS
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting Gateway 503 errors

symptom: 503 from App Service backend behind Application Gateway
rootCause: App Service worker issues, app restarting, or disk mounting problems
solution: 1) Check App Service status. 2) Check metrics: HTTP Server Errors, Memory, CPU. 3) Test appname.azurewebsites.net directly. 4) Check scm endpoint. 5) Collab with App Service team
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting Gateway 503 errors

symptom: Azure Portal metrics/logs show 'We are not able to retrieve these values' for AppGW and other resources subscription-wide
rootCause: Chrome 142 security prompt blocking CORS requests to local network
solution: Change Chrome settings per Chromium bug https://issues.chromium.org/issues/460178100 or use different browser. Browser-side issue, not AppGW
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting Metrics and Logs Unavailability in Application Gateway

symptom: Self-signed certificate uploaded to Application Gateway v2 listener fails validation or is rejected. Customer uses a standalone self-signed cert (not part of a chain) and gets certificate errors.
rootCause: Application Gateway v2 requires certificates to be part of a proper chain (Root CA + Leaf). A standalone self-signed certificate without a chain is not accepted by AppGW v2, unlike v1 which was more lenient.
solution: Create a self-signed Root CA certificate, then generate a leaf certificate signed by that root. Bundle both into a PFX file with the private key. Method 1 (openssl): use separate root.cnf and leaf.cnf config files to generate root + leaf, bundle them, then create PFX. Method 2 (PowerShell): New-SelfSignedCertificate with -CertStoreLocation, create root with CA:TRUE extension, then create leaf with -Signer $rootCert.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Create Self Signed Certs for use with Appgw

symptom: Configuring a second Diagnostic Settings on Application Gateway fails with error: 'Data sink is already used in diagnostic setting for category ApplicationGatewayAccessLog. Data sinks can't be reused in different settings on the same category for the same resource.'
rootCause: Azure does not allow multiple Diagnostic Settings with the same Log category (e.g., ApplicationGatewayAccessLog) pointing to the same Log Analytics workspace, even if the Destination Table type (AzureDiagnostics vs Resource Specific) is different.
solution: Use a single Diagnostic Settings entry per Log category per LAW destination. If you need both AzureDiagnostics and Resource Specific tables, send them to different Log Analytics workspaces, or consolidate into one Diagnostic Settings with the desired Destination Table.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Customer Facing Queries for AGW

symptom: Customer receives 403 Forbidden when WAF is in Prevention mode. Legitimate traffic blocked by OWASP/DRS managed rules. WAF firewall logs show action 'Matched' or 'Blocked' with specific ruleId (e.g., 942260 SQL injection, 920320 missing user-agent).
rootCause: Request data (args, cookies, headers) matches WAF rule regex patterns. Azure WAF uses anomaly scoring model: each rule match adds 2-5 points based on severity (Notice=2, Warning=3, Error=4, Critical=5). When total anomaly score reaches threshold (default 5), mandatory rule 949110 triggers and blocks the request.
solution: 1) Identify the matched field from WAF logs (AGWFirewallLogs or AzureDiagnostics). 2) In the 'data' field, find the value after 'found within' — this shows the match variable (ARGS, REQUEST_COOKIES, REQUEST_HEADERS) and field name. 3) Create exclusion: choose match variable (Request Arg Values/Cookie Values/Header Values), operator (Equals/Contains), and selector (field name). 4) For RCA: get rule regex from OWASP CRS GitHub repo, test with Regex101 or Copilot to explain why match occurred. Note: 'Values' and 'Names' match variables function the same (exclude VALUE based on NAME); use 'Keys' when log shows REQUEST_x_NAMES.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/Troubleshooting/Application Gateway WAF Troubleshooting Guide

symptom: Application Gateway V2 returns 413 Request Entity Too Large even after adjusting WAF custom rules or increasing body size limits. File uploads fail with 413 error.
rootCause: Request body exceeds SecRequestBodyLimit (default 128KB for non-file content) or file upload exceeds limit (default 100MB). Critical: Content-Type header determines which limit applies. If Content-Type is NOT set to multipart/form-data or image/jpeg, file uploads are evaluated against the smaller request body size limit instead of the file upload limit.
solution: 1) Verify Content-Type header is correctly set to multipart/form-data or image/jpeg for file uploads — this triggers the file upload size limit (100MB) instead of request body limit (128KB). 2) Adjust WAF policy settings: Max Request Body Size, Request Body Inspection Limit, File Upload Limit as needed. 3) These settings are only configurable in OWASP 3.2 or later — upgrade if on earlier version. 4) Key WAF body limits: Request Body Check (default true), Request Body Inspection Limit (128KB), Max Request Body Size (128KB), File Upload Limit (100MB).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/Troubleshooting/Application Gateway WAF Troubleshooting Guide

symptom: WAF blocks file uploads with rule ID 200002 (Failed to parse request body), rule 200004 (Possible Multipart Unmatched Boundary), or rule ID 0 (JSON parsing error). Action shows 'Blocked' in WAF firewall logs.
rootCause: Rule 200002: File name or content contains special characters (quotes) that trigger mod_security parser. Rule 200004: Uploaded file content (especially binary like .docx/zip) contains lines starting with dashes that resemble multipart boundary separators. Rule 0: Content-length is 0 or JSON content has invalid characters causing parsing failure.
solution: Rule 200002: Fix Content-Type header to correct MIME type (image/jpeg, etc.) instead of application/json for file uploads. Rule 200004: Disable rule 200004 in WAF policy (now customizable). Rule 0: Validate JSON format using JSONLint. General: Ensure Content-Type accurately reflects the payload type.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/Troubleshooting/Application Gateway WAF Troubleshooting Guide

symptom: When upgrading WAF policy from OWASP 3.2 to DRS 2.1 via Azure Portal, all managed ruleset customizations (rule state overrides, rule actions, rule-level exclusions) are reset to defaults. Only custom rules, policy settings, and global exclusions are preserved.
rootCause: Azure Portal limitation: assigning a new managed ruleset version resets all previous customizations from the existing managed ruleset. This is a documented limitation but poorly surfaced during portal upgrade flow.
solution: Use PowerShell script (updateToDrs21.ps1) to programmatically upgrade while preserving rule overrides and exclusions. Script: 1) Gets rule ID differences between OWASP 3.2 and DRS 2.1, 2) Removes references to rules that exist in OWASP 3.2 but not DRS 2.1, 3) Maps OWASP rule group names to DRS equivalents (e.g., REQUEST-942-APPLICATION-ATTACK-SQLI → SQLI), 4) Preserves all exclusions and rule overrides. Usage: ./updateToDrs21.ps1 -resourceGroupName <rg> -wafPolicyName <policy>. IMPORTANT: Test in non-prod first.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Upgrade Script OWASP 3.2 to DRS 2.1

symptom: Application Gateway Layer 4 TLS proxy backend health probe failures. BackendServerDiagnostic logs show certificate-related errors including cert CN mismatch or root CA trust issues.
rootCause: Two common causes: 1) TLS probe ServerNameIndication (SNI) value does not match backend server certificate CN/SAN. 2) Backend server certificate root CA is not in Application Gateway's trusted root certificate store.
solution: 1) For SNI mismatch: Check the ServerNameIndication property in BackendSettings and ensure it matches the backend certificate CN/SAN. 2) For Root CA issues: Upload the correct trusted root certificate to Application Gateway. 3) Use BackendServerDiagnosticHistory logs (AppGWT table) filtered by SettingName/listenerName/ruleName for detailed probe failure analysis. 4) Verify L4 feature is enabled: check AFEC flags (AllowApplicationGatewayTlsProxy, EnableApplicationGatewayTlsProxyBackendHealth) and L4 properties (ApplicationGatewayListener, RoutingRule, BackendSettings) are non-empty.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Application Gateway Layer 4 Proxy/Layer 4 Application Gateway Troubleshooting Guide

symptom: Configuring a second Diagnostic Settings on Application Gateway fails with error: Data sink is already used in diagnostic setting for category ApplicationGatewayAccessLog. Data sinks cannot be reused in different settings on the same category for the same resource.
rootCause: Azure does not allow multiple Diagnostic Settings with the same Log category (e.g., ApplicationGatewayAccessLog) pointing to the same Log Analytics workspace, even if the Destination Table type (AzureDiagnostics vs Resource Specific) is different.
solution: Use a single Diagnostic Settings entry per Log category per LAW destination. If you need both AzureDiagnostics and Resource Specific tables, send them to different Log Analytics workspaces, or consolidate into one Diagnostic Settings with the desired Destination Table.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Customer Facing Queries for AGW

symptom: AppGW V2 returns 413 Request Entity Too Large. File uploads fail even after adjusting limits.
rootCause: Content-Type determines which limit applies. Wrong Content-Type uses body limit (128KB) instead of file upload limit (100MB).
solution: Set Content-Type to multipart/form-data or image/jpeg. Adjust WAF body/file limits. Requires OWASP 3.2+.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/Troubleshooting/Application Gateway WAF Troubleshooting Guide

symptom: WAF blocks uploads with rule 200002/200004/0.
rootCause: 200002: special chars in filename. 200004: binary content resembles boundary. 0: JSON parse error.
solution: 200002: fix Content-Type. 200004: disable rule. 0: validate JSON.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/Troubleshooting/Application Gateway WAF Troubleshooting Guide

symptom: Portal OWASP 3.2 to DRS 2.1 upgrade resets all WAF customizations.
rootCause: Portal resets managed ruleset customizations on version change.
solution: Use PowerShell updateToDrs21.ps1 to preserve overrides and exclusions.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Upgrade Script OWASP 3.2 to DRS 2.1

symptom: L4 TLS proxy backend health probe failures with cert errors.
rootCause: SNI mismatch with backend cert CN/SAN or root CA not trusted.
solution: Check ServerNameIndication in BackendSettings. Upload trusted root cert. Use BackendServerDiagnosticHistory.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Application Gateway Layer 4 Proxy/Layer 4 Application Gateway Troubleshooting Guide

symptom: AGC CNI Overlay configuration fails when using Kubenet networking in AKS
rootCause: Kubenet is not supported by Application Gateway for Containers; only Azure CNI is supported
solution: Switch from Kubenet to Azure CNI (e.g., Azure CNI for dynamic IP allocation or Azure CNI for static block allocation) when deploying AGC with CNI Overlay
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Features/Feature: CNI Overlay Support

symptom: AGC CNI Overlay provisioning fails or subnet delegation returns error due to incorrect subnet prefix size
rootCause: The Application Gateway for Containers subnet must be exactly /24 prefix. A larger or smaller prefix is not supported
solution: Resize or recreate the AGC subnet to use a /24 prefix and ensure subnet delegation is enabled
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Features/Feature: CNI Overlay Support

symptom: AGC CNI Overlay not working due to ALB controller version incompatibility
rootCause: Minimum ALB controller version required for CNI Overlay support is 1.4.12 (publicly documented preferred version: 1.5.0). Older versions do not support CNI Overlay.
solution: Upgrade the ALB controller to at least version 1.4.12; preferred version is 1.5.0 or later
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Features/Feature: CNI Overlay Support

symptom: AGC migration script (Ingress YAML to Gateway API YAML) produces unexpected output or fails in ManagedBy scenario
rootCause: The migration script assumes the ApplicationLoadBalancer resource has not been created yet in ManagedBy scenarios; if it already exists, the script behavior is unexpected
solution: Ensure the ApplicationLoadBalancer resource has NOT been created before running the migration script for ManagedBy AGC. Migration script is available at: https://github.com/Azure/Application-Gateway-for-Containers-Migration-Utility
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/How To/3- AGC Migration Script and usage (Part 3 of video series)

symptom: openssl pkcs12 command fails or produces errors when extracting private key from a PFX file encrypted with 3DES-SHA1 on systems running OpenSSL version 3.0.0 or newer
rootCause: OpenSSL 3.0+ moved legacy cryptographic algorithms (including 3DES-SHA1) to a separate legacy provider not loaded by default; standard pkcs12 commands cannot process these older PFX encryption formats without explicitly enabling the legacy provider
solution: Add -legacy and -provider-path flags to openssl pkcs12 command: openssl pkcs12 -in example.pfx -nocerts -out key.key -legacy -provider-path C:\path\to\legacy\provider. For AES256-SHA256 encrypted PFX files, no legacy flags are needed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Training - AGC Configuration Lab/03 - ALB Deployment

symptom: Application Gateway for Containers returns HTTP 500 error for all requests even though Gateway and HTTPRoute are configured correctly and backend application pods are running
rootCause: AKS namespace isolation prevents an HTTPRoute in one namespace (e.g., azure-alb-system) from referencing backend Services in a different namespace (e.g., application-deployment) without an explicit ReferenceGrant permission object
