solution: Create a ReferenceGrant object in the backend service namespace: apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1beta1, kind: ReferenceGrant, metadata: {name: <name>, namespace: <backend-ns>}, spec: {from: [{group: gateway.networking.k8s.io, kind: HTTPRoute, namespace: <alb-ns>}], to: [{group: '', kind: Service}]}
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway for Containers (formerly Azure Traffic Controller)/Training - AGC Configuration Lab/04 - Gateway and Route deployment

symptom: Application Gateway Private Link CRUD operation fails with error 'ApplicationGatewayOperationNotSupportedOnV1Sku'
rootCause: Private Link for Application Gateway is only supported on V2 SKU. The operation was attempted on a V1 SKU gateway.
solution: Migrate Application Gateway from V1 to V2 SKU to leverage Private Link capabilities.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting Private Link for Application Gateway

symptom: Provisioning of Private Link Service fails with error: resource name is invalid / name too long (up to 80 characters limit exceeded)
rootCause: Known issue (WI 25954704): When the Application Gateway resource name is long, the auto-generated internal Private Link policy resource name exceeds the 80-character limit for Azure resource names.
solution: Known issue tracked in WI-25954704. Workaround: use a shorter Application Gateway name so the generated private-link-policy name stays within 80 characters. Monitor WI for fix availability.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Troubleshooting Private Link for Application Gateway

symptom: Application Gateway DELETE (or other CRUD operations) fails; GWM logs show 'AADSTS7000112: Application GatewayRP is disabled' and ARM background task fails with MsalServiceException
rootCause: The customer has disabled the GatewayRP enterprise application (app ID 486c78bf-a0f7-45f1-92fd-37215929e116) in their Azure AD tenant. GWM requires authentication from the customer subscription side, and this disabled state blocks the auth flow.
solution: Customer must re-enable the GatewayRP application in their Azure AD tenant: go to Enterprise Applications → find GatewayRP (486c78bf-a0f7-45f1-92fd-37215929e116) → Properties → set 'Enabled for users to sign in' = Yes. Then retry the failed operation. Refer to: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/enterprise-apps/disable-user-sign-in-portal
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/GatewayRP is disabled

symptom: Application Gateway returns 502 to clients after an underlying datapath component upgrade. Access logs show httpStatus=502 and error_info=ERRORINFO_UPSTREAM_INVALID_HEADER.
rootCause: After the upgrade, AppGW enforces stricter RFC compliance. Backend server returns HTTP response headers with names containing spaces or control characters (0x00-0x1f, 0x7f), which are now rejected.
solution: Fix the backend server to return valid HTTP response headers without spaces or control characters in header names. This is a backend application fix required for RFC compliance.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/HTTP Behavior Changes With Application Gateway Upgrade

symptom: Application Gateway returns 400 or 405 to clients after an underlying datapath component upgrade. Access logs show httpStatus=400 and error_info=ERRORINFO_INVALID_HEADER, or httpStatus=405.
rootCause: After the upgrade, AppGW enforces stricter RFC compliance for client requests. 400 triggers include: spaces/control characters in Host header, URI, or any request header name; multiple Host headers; both Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding present; HTTP/1.0 with Transfer-Encoding header. 405 triggers when client sends HTTP CONNECT method.
solution: Fix the client application to send RFC-compliant HTTP requests. Review HAR traces from client side to identify which invalid pattern applies. This is a client-side fix.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/HTTP Behavior Changes With Application Gateway Upgrade

symptom: AppGW v2 CRUD operations fail with error 'ApplicationGatewayILBDeploymentFailureDueToPrivateIPInUse' or 'Unable to reserve private IP address, the private IP address is already in use'. Gateway may enter failed state.
rootCause: The private Frontend IP address chosen by the customer is already allocated to one of the AppGW's internal instances. AppGW instances use IPs from the same subnet; customers are unaware these IPs are reserved. Creating a private frontend IP without a listener does not reserve the IP — it can be claimed by instances during scaling or VMSS repairs.
solution: 1. Check instance IPs via ASC Properties tab and the failed operation details to confirm the IP conflict. 2. If no listener is attached: delete the conflicting private frontend IP config and recreate with a different IP using PowerShell (Remove-AzApplicationGatewayFrontendIPConfig) or CLI. 3. If listener is attached: temporarily move listener to public frontend, delete private frontend IP, recreate with a new IP, then reattach listener. Use IPs from the end of the AppGW subnet range to avoid conflicts.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/AppGW v2 CRUD Operations Failing Due to the ApplicationGatewayILBDeploymentFailureDueToPrivateIPInUse error

symptom: AppGW v2 is in failed/unhealthy state and cannot be recovered. AppGW logs show 'Failed to retrieve secret from KeyVault' errors in KeyVaultSecretRetriever component. ASC insights indicate KV integration error.
rootCause: Key Vault Firewall is configured for Private Endpoint or selected networks only, and AppGW is not included in the allowed access. Requires both: (1) trusted services allowed for control plane cert validation, and (2) AppGW subnet/public IP allowed for data plane cert download.
solution: Configure Key Vault Firewall to 'All Networks' or add two required access rules: (1) Enable 'Allow trusted Microsoft services to bypass this firewall' for control plane access; (2) Add AppGW's subnet or public IP to allowed list for data plane access. Verify DNS resolution for KV hostname is working from AppGW VNet. Reference: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/application-gateway/key-vault-certs
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/AppGW v2 Failed State Due to KeyVault Failure

symptom: AppGW v2 is in failed state due to Key Vault integration. Logs show 'Failed to retrieve secret from KeyVault'. Managed identity is configured on AppGW but certificate retrieval fails.
rootCause: The managed identity configured on Application Gateway is missing from the Key Vault access policy, or has incorrect/insufficient permissions (needs GET permission for secrets and certificates).
solution: Grant the managed identity configured on AppGW the required GET permissions on the Key Vault (under Access Policies → add the managed identity → Secret permissions: Get; Certificate permissions: Get). Verify the correct managed identity is referenced in AppGW SslSecrets configuration.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/AppGW v2 Failed State Due to KeyVault Failure

symptom: AppGW v2 is in failed state due to Key Vault integration. Certificate was deleted from Key Vault but still referenced in AppGW SSL configuration. KeyVaultSecretRetriever logs show retrieval failure.
rootCause: SSL certificate was deleted from Key Vault but the reference remains in AppGW SSL certificates configuration. If the cert is tied to a listener, the gateway cannot function and enters failed state.
solution: Option 1 (cert not tied to listener): Delete the cert reference from AppGW using Remove-AzApplicationGatewaySslCertificate + Set-AzApplicationGateway, or re-add the cert to KV with the same name. Option 2 (cert tied to listener/routing rule): Remove the listener and routing rule first, then remove the cert reference. Option 3 (replace cert on listener without deleting): Use Set-AzApplicationGatewayHttpListener to switch to a valid cert, then remove the bad KV cert reference. Full PowerShell scripts available in wiki.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/AppGW v2 Failed State Due to KeyVault Failure

symptom: Application Gateway v1 returns server errors or unexpected behavior when URLs contain special characters (+, %, :, *, ;, \, &) and HTTP to HTTPS listener redirection is enabled.
rootCause: AppGW v1 SKU does not tolerate '+' character in the URL under any conditions, and does not accept %, :, *, ;, \, & special characters when HTTP-to-HTTPS redirection is enabled. Root cause is ASP.NET limitation in the underlying datapath.
solution: 1. Disable managed runtime version on the backend, or add a web.config allowing these characters. 2. If possible, migrate to AppGW v2 SKU which does not have this limitation. Note: This issue is specific to v1 SKU only.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/AppGtw special characters limitation in the request URL for HTTP to HTTPS Redirection

symptom: Application Gateway backend health shows Unhealthy with 401 responses; probes use anonymous access causing failure against backends requiring NTLM authentication (e.g., SharePoint sites)
rootCause: Application Gateway health probes do not support credential configuration and use anonymous access by default; backends configured with NTLM authentication (e.g., SharePoint) return 401, causing probe health to report Unhealthy
solution: 1) Allow anonymous access on the backend site for the probe path; 2) Configure probe to target a separate path that does not require authentication (not recommended as it bypasses real health checking); 3) Configure probe match to accept 401 as a valid response code via the probe-matching feature (https://docs.microsoft.com/azure/application-gateway/application-gateway-probe-overview#probe-matching)
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/AppGateway getting 401 responses for the probes

symptom: Application Gateway redirect rule appends unexpected trailing slash to target URL (e.g., configured target is https://test.com but AppGW redirects to https://test.com/)
rootCause: The 'Include Path' option is checked and grayed out in the Azure Portal (cannot be disabled via UI), causing AppGW to append a trailing slash when performing URL redirects
solution: Use Azure Resource Explorer to modify the redirect configuration JSON and explicitly set `"includePath": false` to prevent the trailing slash from being added to redirect URLs
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Application Gateway adding a trailing slash when redirecting traffic

symptom: Application Gateway v2 with path-based routing rule returns HTTP 301 redirect when the URL path is missing a trailing slash (e.g., /somepath fails with 301; /somepath/ works correctly)
rootCause: By-design behavior per RFC 3986 Section 6.2.4 (Protocol-Based Normalization) — AppGW v2 path-based rules normalize URLs by enforcing a trailing slash when it is missing from the request path
solution: This is expected by-design behavior. Customer should: 1) Use trailing slash in their URLs; 2) Update application to handle 301 redirects; no configuration option available to disable this behavior
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Application Gateway v2 returns HTTP status 301 if trailing slash is missing

symptom: Portal error when saving Application Gateway configuration: 'Failed to save configuration changes... you have reached 100 redirection rules. The number of redirect configuration is 101 and the maximum allowed is 100'
rootCause: Application Gateway has a hard limit of 100 redirect configurations; customer exceeded the limit by creating separate redirect configs for each path-based rule even when multiple rules share the same redirect destination
solution: Review all redirect configurations and consolidate duplicates — multiple path-based rules pointing to the same redirect destination can share a single redirect configuration. Look for path-based rules that have identical target URLs and merge them to use one shared redirect config
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Common AppGW Portal Errors

symptom: Application Gateway enters Failed State; NRP logs show error: 'Input string was not in a correct format' originating from ValidateCustomErrorBlobAsync / ValidateCustomErrorConfigurationsAsync
rootCause: Application Gateway fails to validate Custom Error Page configuration because the referenced URL is malformed or the hosted error page is inaccessible / returns a non-200 HTTP response code
solution: 1) Fix custom error page: ensure the HTML file is hosted at a publicly accessible URL returning HTTP 200; 2) Remove custom error page config via PowerShell: `Remove-AzApplicationGatewayCustomError` (gateway-level) or `Remove-AzApplicationGatewayHttpListenerCustomError` (listener-level). Use NRP FrontendOperationEtwEvent in Jarvis to confirm the error scenario first
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Foundational and Specialist Troubleshooting/Failed State Input string was not in a correct format

symptom: AppGW returns 429 response when routing traffic through APIM; APIM error 'Rate limit is exceeded'; may appear even when rate-limit policy seems within bounds
rootCause: APIM rate-limit-by-key policy surpassed; OR port 4290 blocked on NSG of APIM subnet (both inbound & outbound) — port 4290 is required for APIM rate-limit enforcement
solution: Check NSG rules on APIM subnet: ensure port 4290 is open both inbound and outbound. If NSG is not the issue, review the rate-limit-by-key policy parameters in APIM.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Application Gateway Layer 4 Proxy/Minimizing Collaboration with APIM from APP GW Side

symptom: AppGW returns 502 Bad Gateway when routing through APIM; APIM logs show BackendConnectionFailure with error 'The remote name could not be resolved: BackendFQDN'
rootCause: Backend FQDN configured in APIM is not resolvable from the APIM subnet — DNS resolution failure in APIM's network context
solution: Verify DNS resolution of backend FQDN from the APIM subnet (check custom DNS servers or Private DNS zones). Use Kusto query on APIM cluster to confirm backendResponseCode=0 and errorReason=BackendConnectionFailure. Ensure routing and NSG allow DNS queries from APIM subnet.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Application Gateway Layer 4 Proxy/Minimizing Collaboration with APIM from APP GW Side

symptom: AppGW returns 500 when routing through APIM; APIM logs show BackendConnectionFailure 'Unable to read data from the Transport connection. The connection was closed'; occurs with POST requests with payload > 2MB when backend has mutual auth enabled
rootCause: POST request payload exceeds 2MB with mutual TLS (mTLS) enabled on backend, causing the backend to close the transport stream prematurely during payload transfer
solution: Review mTLS configuration on backend for large payload handling. Consider increasing connection timeout or payload size limits. Check if backend server has TLS buffer size constraints. Engage backend team to diagnose why large POST with mTLS causes connection drop.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Application Gateway Layer 4 Proxy/Minimizing Collaboration with APIM from APP GW Side

symptom: AppGW backend pool pointing to AKS shows unhealthy with probe timeout error: 'Time taken by the backend to respond to application gateway's health probe is more than the time-out threshold'; AKS cluster logs show AADSTS7000222: InvalidClientSecretExpiredKeysProvided
rootCause: The AAD Service Principal client secret used by the AKS cluster has expired (error AADSTS7000222: InvalidClientSecretExpiredKeysProvided). AKS cannot authenticate and therefore cannot respond to AppGW health probe requests.
solution: 1. Get AKS credentials: `az aks get-credentials --resource-group <rg> --name <cluster>`. 2. Find the expired secret: `kubectl get secret -A | grep <secret-name>`. 3. Decode the base64 secret: `kubectl get secret <secret-name> -o yaml` then `echo '<base64-value>' | base64 --decode` to extract clientId and clientSecret. 4. Reset the credential: `az ad sp credential reset --name <clientId> --password <clientSecret> --years 100`. 5. Wait 15-30 minutes for AKS to pick up the updated credential.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Backend Unhealthy: AKS Backend Pool with client secret keys expired

symptom: Customer struggles to upload or download large files through AppGW v2 Standard SKU; slow transfer speeds, connection drops, or timeouts on large payload operations
rootCause: Request and response proxy buffering is enabled by default on AppGW v2 Standard SKU. This buffering can cause issues with large file uploads/downloads as the gateway attempts to buffer the entire payload before forwarding.
solution: Disable request and response proxy buffering via REST API PUT on the AppGW globalConfiguration property. Use resources.azure.com/raw (GET the config, add `"globalConfiguration":{"enableRequestBuffering":false,"enableResponseBuffering":false}`, then PUT) or Azure API Playground (portal: https://ms.portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Resources/ArmPlayground for public, https://portal.azure.us/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Resources/ArmPlayground for Gov). Note: this setting is NOT configurable via PowerShell or CLI.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Configure Request and Response Proxy Buffers via API Playground or resources.azure.com

symptom: Requests to root URL path '/' on AppGW are not forwarded to the intended backend path; AppGW path-based rules cannot be created for '/' path (error: 'The path must start with a forward slash')
rootCause: AppGW path-based routing rules do not support '/' as a standalone path entry. Requests to the site root (empty path or '/') require a rewrite rule approach rather than a path-based rule.
solution: Create a rewrite rule set on the AppGW: 1. Condition: match URL path with regex `^/$` (use ^ and $ anchors to match ONLY root path, not all paths starting with /). 2. Action: set URL path to the desired backend path (e.g., `/defaultpage`). This ensures only root path requests are rewritten while other paths are forwarded unchanged.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Forward Requests With Empty Paths To Specific Path

symptom: HTTP 404 error when accessing Application Gateway via a hostname different from the one configured in the HTTP/S Listener
rootCause: When a hostname value is set in the AppGw HTTP Listener, the gateway only responds to requests matching that hostname via ARR binding. Requests with a different hostname receive HTTP 404 (no site bound to that hostname). With HTTPS, a certificate hostname mismatch error appears first.
solution: Remove the hostname value from the AppGw listener so it accepts all hostnames, or access the gateway exclusively via the configured hostname.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/How To: Resolve HTTP 404 error when declaring hostname in listener

symptom: When Application Gateway is in front of an Azure Web App, a URL redirect causes the browser to show the backend Web App URL (*.azurewebsites.net) instead of the AppGw frontend domain — exposing the backend address to clients
rootCause: AppGw sends the backend hostname (e.g., appgwtest.azurewebsites.net) in the HTTP Host header to the Web App. When the Web App returns a 301 redirect, the Location header contains the backend hostname. AppGw passes this response back to the client unmodified, causing the browser to redirect to the backend URL.
solution: Remove -PickHostnameFromBackendAddress / -PickHostnameFromBackendHttpSettings from AppGw config. Configure AppGw to forward the original client hostname intact to the Web App. Configure a custom domain on the Web App matching the AppGw frontend domain, and update DNS CNAME accordingly.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/How to: Setup URL Redirection with WebApp Backend

symptom: Application Gateway returns 504 Gateway Timeout at exactly 60 seconds regardless of HTTP settings timeout configuration. Multiple backends marked unhealthy causing 502 Bad Gateway errors. CPU and memory remain normal during the incident.
rootCause: SNAT (Source Network Address Translation) port exhaustion on the Application Gateway. Occurs when the gateway runs out of available SNAT ports for outbound connections to public-facing backend pools. AppGw does not auto-scale based on SNAT ports.
solution: Option 1: Increase minimum instance count — each instance provides 1024 SNAT ports. Option 2: Deploy a NAT Gateway associated with the AppGw subnet so outbound traffic uses NAT Gateway's public IP instead. Option 3: Use private IP addresses / Private Link / Private Endpoints for backends to avoid SNAT port usage altogether. Diagnosis: Check snatAvailability in VMSS_Platform Dashboard; look for Snat connection-Pending and Snat connection-Failed entries matching the 504/502 timestamps.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/How to Identify and Mitigate SNAT Exhaustion in Azure Application Gateway

symptom: Azure portal does not allow selecting a Key Vault Secret (from the Secrets vault) as an SSL certificate for Application Gateway listener — only certificates from the Certificates vault are shown in the portal UI
rootCause: Portal integration for AppGw SSL certificate configuration only exposes the Certificates vault pool, not secrets. Customers who imported certificates to Key Vault as secrets (not certificates) cannot configure AppGw SSL termination via the portal.
solution: Use PowerShell to add the SSL certificate using the Key Vault SecretId directly: (1) Get the AppGw object, (2) Use Add-AzApplicationGatewaySslCertificate with -KeyVaultSecretId pointing to the secret URL, (3) Assign a User Managed Identity with Key Vault 'get secrets' permission, (4) Run Set-AzApplicationGateway. After running the script, the secret becomes selectable in the portal listener configuration.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/How to use secrets-certs from Key Vault for AppGw SSL termination Powershell script

symptom: New-AzApplicationGateway PowerShell cmdlet fails consistently when both -Identity (managed identity) and -FirewallPolicy or -FirewallPolicyId parameters are specified together in a single command
rootCause: Known limitation in the New-AzApplicationGateway cmdlet: the -Identity parameter does not exist within the same parameter set as -FirewallPolicy and -FirewallPolicyId. This is a PowerShell SDK bug tracked by PG. ANP tracking item: https://supportability.visualstudio.com/AzureNetworking/_workitems/edit/74969
solution: Two-step workaround: (1) Create the V2 AppGw using Standard_v2 SKU with the -Identity parameter (no WAF policy). (2) After creation, retrieve the AppGw, assign the WAF policy ($appgw.firewallpolicy = $wafPolicy), update the SKU to WAF_v2, and run Set-AzApplicationGateway.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/How to work around Azure Powershell limitations when creating a V2 WAF Application Gateway with a Key Vault managed identity and a WAF policy

symptom: AppGW 配置 HTTP→HTTPS 重定向，目标 listener 为 multisite listener（含多个 hostname）时，客户端收到 301 响应但无法访问站点；Location 响应头包含 multisite listener 所有 hostname 的逗号拼接字符串（如 https://host1.com,host2.com,host3.com/），导致浏览器无法跳转
rootCause: AppGW 不支持将重定向目标设置为 multisite listener。将多站点 HTTPS listener 作为重定向目标时，AppGW 将该 listener 上配置的所有 hostname 以逗号拼接后填入 Location 头，属于已知限制
solution: 将重定向目标改为 single-site listener（单一 hostname）。若需对多个 hostname 分别重定向，需为每个 hostname 创建独立的单站点 listener 并分别配置重定向规则
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Redirection Issue with the multisite listener

symptom: 通过 AppGW V1 访问后端 Web 服务时响应延迟超过 10 分钟甚至超时；或第三方应用（如 Excel）通过 AppGW V1 获取报表时出现 'Invalid XML file received' 错误或响应不记录；直接访问后端 Web 服务器则正常
rootCause: AppGW V1（基于 IIS）默认启用 Dynamic Compression。客户端请求携带 Accept-Encoding: gzip 时，即使后端返回未压缩内容，AppGW V1 也会将响应重新压缩后返回给客户端，导致额外延迟。AppGW V2 不具备此 Dynamic Compression 行为
solution: 方案1：去掉客户端请求中的 Accept-Encoding: gzip 头，避免触发 AppGW V1 压缩。方案2（推荐）：迁移到 Application Gateway V2，V2 不会对响应进行 Dynamic Compression
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Response from Application Gateway V1 delayed due to compression

symptom: AppGW 同时配置了 wildcard listener（如 *.domain.com）和特定 hostname listener（如 webapp.domain.com），请求 webapp.domain.com 时被路由到 wildcard listener 对应的后端，而非预期的特定 hostname listener 后端
rootCause: wildcard listener 对应路由规则的 priority 数值低于特定 hostname listener 的路由规则（数值越小优先级越高）。AppGW 按优先级顺序匹配，wildcard 规则优先匹配，webapp.domain.com 完全匹配 *.domain.com，导致走了错误的路由
solution: 将特定 hostname listener（如 webapp.domain.com）对应路由规则的 priority 数值设置为低于 wildcard listener 的路由规则数值（例如特定 hostname 用 priority 10，wildcard 用 priority 1000），使特定 hostname 规则先于 wildcard 规则被评估
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Routing Issue With Wildcard listener Due to Priority

symptom: AppGW绑定了KeyVault证书，但KeyVault中的证书更新后AppGW不自动同步新版本，仍继续使用已过期或旧版本的证书
rootCause: Set-AzApplicationGatewaySslCertificate 使用了带有具体版本号的 SecretId（如 .../secrets/certname/abc123）；当 SecretId 包含版本号时，AppGW只固定引用该特定版本，不会自动检测KV中的新版本
solution: 使用无版本号的 SecretId：$secretId = $secret.Id.Replace($secret.Version, "")，使SecretId格式变为 .../secrets/certname/（末尾斜杠，无版本）。AppGW此后会自动检测并下载KV中的最新版本证书。官方文档明确说明：若需AppGW与KeyVault同步证书，须提供无版本号的 secretId
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Keyvault secrets do not update on appgw

symptom: AppGW后端健康探测返回401 Unauthorized，后端被标记为不健康（Unhealthy），但后端App Service本身运行正常；或健康探测反复失败，实际业务流量正常
rootCause: AppGW健康探测是简单HTTP请求，不携带Authentication headers/token/cookie；后端App Service配置了Entra ID (AAD)认证，导致所有未认证请求（包括健康探测）收到401响应
solution: 方案1（快速）：将AppGW健康探测配置为接受HTTP 401作为健康响应码，适用于所有端点均需认证的情况，401确认服务在运行。方案2（推荐）：在App Service创建专用健康检查路径（如/health.html），通过az rest --method put更新authsettingsV2的excludedPaths将该路径排除在Entra ID认证之外，将AppGW探测指向该路径并期望200 OK响应
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Mitigating App GW Health Probe 401 error Issues with Authentication enabled App Service

symptom: 删除Application Gateway失败，错误：Cannot delete application gateway because its child resources are in use by virtual machine scale set
rootCause: VMSS的NIC网络配置（networkInterfaceConfigurations）中的applicationGatewayBackendAddressPools引用了该AppGW的后端池，必须先移除此引用才能删除AppGW
solution: 执行以下Azure CLI命令移除VMSS对AppGW后端池的引用：(1) az vmss update --resource-group <rg> --name <vmss> --remove virtualMachineProfile.networkProfile.networkInterfaceConfigurations[0].ipConfigurations[0].applicationGatewayBackendAddressPools；(2) az vmss update-instances --instance-ids "*" --resource-group <rg> --name <vmss>。完成后再删除AppGW
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Modify an Application Gateway when there is a backend pool referenced in a VMSS

symptom: 将VMSS添加到AppGW后端池时操作报错，随后VMSS更新失败，错误：Resource referenced by resource was not found. Please make sure that the referenced resource exists, and that both resources are in the same region
rootCause: VMSS的NIC配置中残留了对一个已被清空或不存在的AppGW后端池的引用，导致VMSS更新失败
solution: 使用CLI移除VMSS中的孤立引用：(1) az vmss update --resource-group <rg> --name <vmss> --remove virtualMachineProfile.networkProfile.networkInterfaceConfigurations[0].ipConfigurations[0].applicationGatewayBackendAddressPools；(2) az vmss update-instances --instance-ids "*" --resource-group <rg> --name <vmss>。引用移除后，再重新将VMSS加入AppGW后端池
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Modify an Application Gateway when there is a backend pool referenced in a VMSS

symptom: AppGW CRUD操作失败进入Failed状态，错误码：ApplicationGatewayProbeProtocolMustMatchBackendHttpSettinsProtocol；NRP日志显示：Probe protocol (Http) does not match Backend Http Setting protocol (Https)
rootCause: 健康探测（Probe）配置的协议（如HTTP）与关联的后端HTTP设置（Backend HTTP Settings）的协议（如HTTPS）不匹配，NRP验证层拒绝该配置
solution: 简单的Get/Set操作无法修复Failed状态，必须按以下步骤修复协议不匹配：(1) 删除请求路由规则（Request Routing Rule）；(2) 删除错误信息中引用的Backend HTTP Settings；(3) 重新创建HTTP规则和Backend HTTP Settings，确保Probe与Backend HTTP Settings使用相同协议（均为HTTP或均为HTTPS）
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Probe and HTTP Setting Protocol Mismatch

symptom: 使用PowerShell将AppGW移动到同VNet内新子网时失败，错误：(a) private IP cannot be changed；(b) frontend subnet and gateway subnet can not be different；(c) IP allocation type cannot be changed - allocation type is Static and cannot be changed to Dynamic
rootCause: AppGW V2（含私有前端IP）、Private-only V2 AppGW、或V1（静态私有前端IP）无法直接跨子网移动；只有动态IP的私有前端配置支持直接移动；静态私有IP配置不允许在移动过程中变更分配类型
solution: 方案A（间接移动）：(1) 创建公共前端IP配置；(2) 将所有监听器关联到公共前端IP；(3) 删除静态私有前端IP配置；(4) 执行标准子网移动命令（Stop-AzApplicationGateway → Set-AzApplicationGatewayIPConfiguration → Set-AzApplicationGateway）；(5) 创建新的静态私有前端IP；(6) 重新关联监听器；(7) 删除临时公共前端IP。方案B：直接在新子网重建AppGW
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/HowTo: move Application Gateway to new Subnet within the Vnet

symptom: AppGW V1进入Failed状态，无法执行任何配置变更（PUT操作均失败）；通常发生在频繁添加Authentication Certificates之后，或证书数量接近/超过限制时
rootCause: AppGW V1的所有Authentication Certificates总大小有128KB硬限制；持续添加新证书（而非替换现有证书）超过该限制会导致AppGW进入Failed状态。另外V1/V2均有每类证书最多100个的数量限制。证书从监听器/后端设置解除关联后，证书配置并不会自动从AppGW中删除
solution: 从Jarvis Actions获取AppGW raw config JSON文件（Get Application Gateway action）保存为config.json，使用Get-AppGWUnusedCerts.ps1工具识别未使用的证书：(1) .\Get-AppGWUnusedCerts.ps1 -File config.json（查看未使用证书列表）；(2) .\Get-AppGWUnusedCerts.ps1 -File config.json -Out removecerts.ps1（生成删除脚本）；将生成的脚本提供给客户在CloudShell中执行，一次性删除所有未使用证书
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/Identify Unused Application Gateway Certificates - TOOL!

symptom: 上传SSL证书（PFX）到AppGW时失败，错误：Data too big for certificate <AppGW resource ID>/sslCertificates/<cert name>. Max data length supported is 16384
rootCause: AppGW SSL证书有16384字节（16KB）的大小硬限制；文件系统显示的文件大小可能小于16KB，但Base64编码后的证书数据（$cert.Data.length）超过了16384字节
solution: 在PowerShell/CloudShell中验证实际大小：(1) $cert=New-AzApplicationGatewaySslCertificate -Name <name>.pfx -CertificateFile <path>/<file>.pfx；(2) $cert.Data.length。若输出值>16384，证书过大无法上传。需减小证书大小（如移除多余的证书链条目）或重新生成更小的证书
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/How to work with Certs for application gateway

symptom: Azure Portal或CLI上AppGW的证书信息（到期日期、Common Name等元数据）不显示或显示为空白
rootCause: AppGW不在本地存储证书元数据，每次查看时通过GetListenerCertificateMetaData操作从KeyVault实时获取；以下情况会导致获取失败：(1) 证书未绑定到任何监听器；(2) 证书绑定的监听器未关联到路由规则（非活跃监听器）；(3) Managed Identity的KeyVault访问权限失效或变更；(4) Managed Identity权限已变更但尚未传播生效；(5) AppGW处于Stopped状态
solution: 按以下顺序排查：检查证书是否已绑定到监听器 → 确认监听器是否已关联到Request Routing Rule → 检查Managed Identity的KeyVault访问权限是否有效（在Control Plane Dashboard或ASC的Operations标签中查看GetListenerCertificateMetaData操作状态）→ 确认AppGW不处于Stopped状态
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/How to work with Certs for application gateway

symptom: AppGW证书操作（如openssl pkcs12）在使用OpenSSL 3.0.0+版本时失败，错误与RC2-40-CBC或3DES-SHA1加密算法有关
rootCause: OpenSSL 3.0.0起弃用了RC2-40-CBC等旧加密算法支持；Windows默认使用3DES-SHA1导出PFX，这些算法在OpenSSL 3.x+中需要额外的legacy provider flags才能处理
solution: 在openssl命令中添加 -legacy 和 -provider-path 参数：openssl pkcs12 -info -nokeys -in cert.pfx -legacy -provider-path "C:\path\to\legacy.dll"。或者在Windows中导出PFX时改用AES256-SHA256加密（参考：https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-server/identity/ad-cs/export-certificate-private-key）
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/How To/How to work with Certs for application gateway

symptom: OnDemand Health Probe (ODHP) marks backend as unhealthy when backend server certificate chain includes intermediate certificates but customer only uploaded root certificate as Trusted Root Cert in Backend HTTP Setting
rootCause: ODHP uses a different certificate validation path from standard probes; it only loads Trusted Root Certs into the trust store and does not pass intermediate certs to the chain builder, causing chain validation timeout and failure (bug msazure#7258041). Fix planned for Q4 CY2025.
solution: Use standard 'Get Backend Health' API/diagnostics instead of ODHP for accurate backend health results. Steps: (1) Confirm cert chain structure (leaf + intermediates + root); (2) Verify only root cert is in Trusted Root Cert of Backend HTTP Setting; (3) Advise customer to use Get Backend Health until ODHP fix ships in Q4 2025.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Known Issue: OnDemand Health Probe Failure

symptom: Application Gateway continues routing to stale/old backend IP addresses after DNS record changes for FQDN-based backend pool members; traffic fails or routes incorrectly; only recovers after gateway restart or a redundant PUT operation
rootCause: Prior behavior: Nginx only performed DNS resolution at startup and cached IPs for the entire lifetime of the worker process. No periodic DNS refresh was performed, so DNS record changes were unknown to the gateway until it restarted.
solution: Current behavior (resolved in platform update): AppGW now refreshes DNS entries upon TTL expiry. If fresh DNS lookup fails, last-known good IP is maintained and retried every 1 second. If customer is on older AppGW version, trigger a gateway restart or perform a redundant PUT operation to force DNS re-resolution.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Refresh Backend DNS Cache

symptom: Backend health shows unhealthy with error: 'The Common Name of the leaf certificate presented by the backend server does not match the Probe or Backend Setting hostname' (V2) or 'The Common Name (CN) of the backend certificate doesn't match' (V1)
rootCause: The SNI hostname used during TLS handshake (derived from Custom Probe's host field or Backend Setting hostname) does not match the Common Name (CN) in the backend server's leaf certificate.
solution: V2: (a) Default Probe — set hostname in Backend Setting via 'Override with specific hostname' or 'Pick hostname from backend target'; (b) Custom Probe — set correct CN in probe 'host' field or choose 'Pick hostname from backend setting'. V1: Verify backend pool target FQDN matches the cert CN. To find CN: check browser certificate padlock, use certlm.msc on Windows, or run 'openssl x509 -in certificate.crt -text -noout' on Linux.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Revised Backend Health Errors for Certificate issues

symptom: Backend health error: 'The Leaf certificate is not the topmost certificate in the chain presented by the backend server. Ensure the certificate chain is correctly ordered on the backend server.'
rootCause: The leaf (domain/server) certificate is not installed as the first/topmost certificate in the chain on the backend server; chain is incorrectly ordered.
solution: Install the certificate chain on the backend server in correct order: Leaf certificate first (depth 0), then Intermediate certificate(s) (depth 1+), then Root CA certificate last. Verify chain order using: 's_client -connect <FQDN>:443 -showcerts' or 's_client -connect <IP>:443 -servername <SNI_hostname> -showcerts'.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Revised Backend Health Errors for Certificate issues

symptom: Backend health error: 'The Leaf certificate is missing from the certificate chain presented by the backend server. Ensure the chain is complete and correctly ordered on the backend server.'
rootCause: The leaf (domain/server) certificate is missing from the certificate chain installed on the backend server.
solution: Obtain the leaf certificate from your Certificate Authority (CA). Install the complete chain on the backend server in correct order: Leaf → Intermediate(s) → Root CA. Verify with 's_client -connect <FQDN>:443 -showcerts'.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Revised Backend Health Errors for Certificate issues

symptom: Backend health error: 'The Intermediate certificate is missing from the certificate chain presented by the backend server.' Also triggered by self-signed certificates that are not Certificate Authorities.
rootCause: Intermediate certificate(s) not installed in the certificate chain on the backend server. For self-signed certs: AppGW treats a non-CA self-signed cert as a 'Leaf' and expects a signing Intermediate cert, which is absent.
solution: (1) Get intermediate certificate(s) from your CA and install complete chain on backend server: Leaf → Intermediate(s) → Root CA. (2) For self-signed certs: follow correct creation steps at https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/application-gateway/self-signed-certificates to create a self-signed cert that includes CA extensions.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Revised Backend Health Errors for Certificate issues

symptom: Backend health error: 'The backend server certificate is not signed by a well-known Certificate Authority (CA). To use unknown CA certificates, its Root certificate must be uploaded to the Backend Setting of the application gateway.'
rootCause: 'Well-known CA certificate' is selected in the Backend Setting but the backend server's Root CA is a private/unknown CA not in the public trust store.
solution: In the Backend Setting, change to 'not a well-known CA' and upload the Root CA certificate (.CER file). To export the root cert: follow steps at https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/application-gateway/certificates-for-backend-authentication#export-trusted-root-certificate-for-v2-sku
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Revised Backend Health Errors for Certificate issues

symptom: Backend health error: 'The Intermediate certificate is not signed by a well-known Certificate Authority (CA). Ensure the certificate chain is complete and correctly ordered on the backend server.'
rootCause: 'Well-known CA certificate' is selected in the Backend Setting but the Intermediate certificate presented by the backend server is signed by a private/non-public CA.
solution: Contact your private CA to get the appropriate Root CA certificate (.CER). In the Backend Setting, choose 'not a well-known CA' and upload the Root CA cert. Also install the complete chain on the backend server (Leaf → Intermediate(s) → Root CA) for easy verification.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Revised Backend Health Errors for Certificate issues

symptom: Backend health error: 'The Intermediate certificate not signed by any Root certificates uploaded to the application gateway. Ensure the certificate chain is complete and correctly ordered on the backend server.'
rootCause: None of the Root CA certificates uploaded to the Backend Setting have signed the Intermediate certificate on the backend server. Backend server only has leaf and intermediate certs installed (Root CA is missing from the chain).
solution: Contact your private CA to obtain the Root CA certificate (.CER file). Upload it to the Backend Setting of the application gateway. This Root CA must be the one that signed the Intermediate certificate in the backend server's chain.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Revised Backend Health Errors for Certificate issues

symptom: AppGW v2 (Standard_V2) throughput is limited when customers upload or download large files; adding more instances does not increase throughput
rootCause: Standard_V2 AppGW SKU is based on a customized Standard_D1_V2 VM with ~750 Mbps bandwidth cap per instance; bandwidth is inherently limited and horizontal scaling does not increase per-connection throughput
solution: Route large file traffic (e.g. download URLs like dwnld.contoso.com) through CDN, Azure Front Door, or directly to the backend. Route regular traffic via AppGW. Backend must have direct internet connectivity; plan firewall/security compliance accordingly.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Throughput via ApplicationGatewayv2

symptom: VMSS marked for deletion cannot be deleted or modified; all operations fail with error code OperationNotAllowedOnVMScaleSetMarkedForDeletion; underlying AppGW returns ApplicationGatewayErrorApplyingConfiguration
rootCause: AppGW in the VMSS backend pool has an SSL certificate linked to a Key Vault secret that is not DNS-resolvable (KV hostname not reachable); AppGW config validation fails during any operation, blocking all dependent VMSS operations
solution: Option 1: Delete the entire resource group containing both AppGW and VMSS (ensure other resources are no longer needed). If VMSS is stuck, run: Remove-AzVmss -ResourceGroupName '<RG>' -VMScaleSetName '<VMSS>'. Option 2: Navigate to Azure Portal > Application Gateway > Listeners > Listener TLS Certificates, edit the failing certificate and upload a new certificate directly instead of referencing Key Vault.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Unable to delete VMSS

symptom: Backend Last Byte Response Time (BLBRT) metric appears greater than Application Gateway Total Time (AGTT) metric in the metrics dashboard, which seems counterintuitive
rootCause: AGTT is calculated per listener (total time / total requests per listener); BLBRT is calculated per listener/upstream/server and aggregated as a sum across backend pools — different dimensions cause the mathematical discrepancy when comparing aggregated values
solution: View AGTT per listener for an accurate picture. View BLBRT broken down per listener + backend pool + backend HTTP settings + backend server address. Avoid comparing these metrics at different aggregation levels. Large time frames also increase sample intervals making graphs less accurate.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Understanding discrepancies in Application Gateway Metrics

symptom: AppGW CRUD/PUT operations fail with NrpException: 'Problem occurred while accessing and validating KeyVault Secrets associated with Application Gateway'; KMS logs show 'certificate not found in this key vault'
rootCause: AppGW has orphaned TLS certificate references in Listener TLS Certificates section pointing to KeyVault certificates that have already been deleted; AppGW attempts to validate all referenced certificates during any PUT operation, causing failure
solution: Use Vulcan JIT to access the customer's portal. Navigate to AppGW > Listeners > Listener TLS Certificates. Identify and delete all certificates that have 0 associated listeners — these are orphaned references. Delete them one by one until AppGW returns to Succeeded state.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Unused Listener TLS Certificates

symptom: Azure Application Gateway V1 has missing or dropped diagnostics logs intermittently
rootCause: AppGW V1 SKU has a hard platform limit of 100 active HTTP listeners (Active = Total listeners minus listeners not routing traffic); exceeding this limit causes dropped diagnostics logs and incomplete telemetry
solution: Check active listener count in Azure Support Center. If count exceeds 100: remove unused/inactive listeners, consolidate by using wildcard hostnames, or reuse existing listeners. Note: this limit cannot be increased on V1. Consider migrating to V2 SKU if more than 100 active listeners are needed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/V1 Known Issue Missing Logs

symptom: AppGW create or update operations fail with ApplicationGatewayInsufficientPermissionOnSubnet error; also affects AGIC managed identity operations on AppGW
rootCause: The user, service principal, or managed identity performing the AppGW operation lacks the Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/subnets/join/action permission on the VNet/subnet where AppGW is deployed; this check was enabled starting June 2023
solution: Assign the built-in 'Network Contributor' role (or a custom role with Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/subnets/join/action) to the user/SP/MI on the VNet or subnet. For AGIC add-on: assign Network Contributor to the AGIC MI on the AppGW subnet. Temporary workaround: register the AFEC flag 'ApplicationGatewaySubnetPermissionCheck' to disable validation — remove before June 2023 cutoff (no longer available permanently).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/VNET Permission Check

symptom: AGIC pod logs show LinkedAuthorizationFailed error; AGIC cannot perform write/CRUD operations on AppGW after Key Vault integration with a user-assigned managed identity was added to the gateway
rootCause: AGIC managed identity lacks the Microsoft.ManagedIdentity/userAssignedIdentities/assign/action permission on the AppGW's user-assigned managed identity; this permission is required when AppGW uses a managed identity for KV integration, and is typically missed when KV integration is added after initial AGIC deployment
solution: 1. Assign 'Managed Identity Operator' role to the AGIC MI on the AppGW managed identity scope: az role assignment create --role 'Managed Identity Operator' --assignee <agicMIPrincipalId> --scope <appGwManagedIdentityResourceId>. 2. Force AGIC pod token refresh by deleting and recreating the AGIC pod: kubectl delete pod -n <AGICNamespace> <AGICPODNAME>. Entra ID tokens have long lifespan so RBAC changes do not apply until token refresh.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/AGIC Application Gateway Ingress Controller/AGIC is no longer able to run operations after key vault integration is added

symptom: AGIC with Azure CNI Overlay: backend pool shows Pod IP addresses from overlay network; traffic routing fails or backend is unreachable; AGIC deployment fails or behaves unexpectedly after enabling CNI Overlay on AKS
rootCause: Azure CNI Overlay has specific constraints: (1) Only a single V2 Application Gateway per /24 subnet is allowed — larger subnets are unsupported; (2) AGIC 1.8.0+ is required (1.8.1 fixes known controller bug); (3) Global and Regional VNet peerings are not supported; (4) Fairfax and Mooncake regions are not supported (as of July 2025). Without meeting these constraints, routing via the overlay network fails.
solution: Ensure: (1) AppGw subnet is /24 and only one V2 AppGw is deployed on it; (2) Upgrade AGIC to 1.8.1+; (3) Remove any VNet peerings; (4) Do not use with Fairfax/Mooncake. Backend pool IP addresses will be Pod IPs (overlay range) which Application Gateway routes via injected routes — this is expected behavior.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/AGIC Application Gateway Ingress Controller/AGIC with Azure CNI Overlay

symptom: AGIC appears to ignore Ingress health probe annotations; Application Gateway health probe configuration does not match annotations set on Ingress resource; backend shows unhealthy despite seemingly correct annotation settings
rootCause: AGIC probe priority: Pod probe > Ingress annotations > AGIC default. If a readiness or liveness probe is defined in the Pod/Deployment spec, AGIC uses that Pod probe configuration to generate the App Gateway probe and completely ignores all Ingress health probe annotations. This is the most common cause of 'annotations not working' — a Pod probe was defined (possibly months earlier) and is silently taking precedence.
solution: Run `kubectl get deploy -n <namespace> -o yaml` and check for `readinessProbe` or `livenessProbe`. If found, AGIC is using the Pod probe. To change probe behavior: (1) Update the Pod probe definition in the Deployment spec, or (2) Remove the Pod probe and use Ingress annotations instead. Ingress annotations are only active when no Pod probes exist.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/AGIC Application Gateway Ingress Controller/Troubleshooting Health Probes

symptom: Application Gateway operation fails with error: ApplicationGatewayProbePathIsInvalid — 'Path specified for Probe ... is not valid. Path must start with a forward slash (/)'
rootCause: Kubernetes readiness or liveness probe configured with a path that does not start with `/` (e.g., `test.html`). Kubernetes accepts this configuration and the Pod runs fine. However, when AGIC translates the Pod probe to an Application Gateway health probe, Application Gateway requires all probe paths to start with `/`. The missing slash causes the ARM deployment/update to fail.
solution: Run `kubectl get deploy -n <namespace> -o yaml`. Find the readinessProbe or livenessProbe and correct the path to start with `/` (e.g., change `test.html` to `/test.html`). After fixing, AGIC will regenerate the Application Gateway probe with the valid path and the deployment will succeed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/AGIC Application Gateway Ingress Controller/Troubleshooting Health Probes

symptom: WebSocket connections dropped mid-session; large file uploads fail unexpectedly with connection resets; long-lived HTTP connections (streaming, polling APIs) interrupted — specifically when using AGIC-managed Application Gateway
rootCause: AGIC monitors Kubernetes resources and triggers a PUT operation on Application Gateway whenever any Kubernetes config changes. Every PUT operation on Application Gateway triggers a reload of the NGINX service, which resets all existing connections. With AGIC continuously applying configuration updates, connection resets are more frequent than with manually managed Application Gateways.
solution: Migrate to Application Gateway for Containers (AGC) as an alternative. AGC is designed to better support WebSockets, large file uploads, and long-lived connections by avoiding connection disruptions caused by configuration reloads. As a workaround for AGIC: minimize unnecessary Kubernetes resource changes that trigger AGIC reconciliation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/AGIC Application Gateway Ingress Controller/WebSockets, long-Lived Connections and Large file upload

symptom: Application Gateway SSL certificate does not automatically update/rotate after the certificate is renewed in Azure Key Vault; gateway continues to use old/expired certificate version even after renewal
rootCause: Terraform configuration uses the `azurerm_key_vault_secret` data source which includes the specific secret **version** in the Key Vault URL. Application Gateway only supports automatic rotation for **versionless** Key Vault secret references. When the cert is renewed in Key Vault, a new version is created, but the AppGW still points to the old versioned URL.
solution:
Use Terraform's `replace()` function to strip the version from the Key Vault secret URL, creating a versionless reference:

`key_vault_secret_id = replace(data.azurerm_key_vault_secret.vault.id, "/secrets/(.*)/[^/]+/", "secrets/$1")`

This converts the versioned URL (e.g., `.../secrets/mycert/5cd21fe4...`) to a versionless URL (e.g., `.../secrets/mycert`), enabling automatic certificate rotation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Terraform Scenarios/KeyVault Versionless Secrets Using Terraform

symptom: Application Gateway packet capture filters (IP/port) configured via ASC are not being applied; capture includes all traffic instead of filtered traffic
rootCause: Known bug in Application Gateway packet capture functionality via ASC: filters are not applying (Known Bug/Issue 153524 — AppGW packet capture filters not applying).
solution: Workaround: Start packet capture without filters (capture all traffic), then filter the downloaded pcap file post-collection using Wireshark or similar tool. Use minimal capture duration and force-stop early to limit file size.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Tools and TSGs/App Gateway Packet Capture via ASC and Retrieval (Renewed)

symptom: Application Gateway backend shows Unknown health status; backend FQDN cannot be resolved by application gateway instances
rootCause: Missing DNS records for the backend FQDN; blocked connectivity to custom DNS server; private DNS zone missing virtual network link to AppGW VNet
solution: Add/correct DNS records for backend FQDN; fix connectivity to custom DNS server; add VNet link for private DNS zone to AppGW VNet. Root Cause: Windows Azure\Azure DNS Services\...
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Tools and TSGs/BackendConnectivityTSG

symptom: Application Gateway v2 backend server shows unhealthy; private IP backend is unreachable
rootCause: Private IP backend does not exist in the AppGW VNet or its peered VNets; missing VNet peering that was not added
solution: Verify that the backend private IP is within the AppGW VNet address space or a peered VNet; add missing VNet peering. Root Cause: Windows Azure\Virtual Network\VNet Peering\Configuration\How to
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Tools and TSGs/BackendConnectivityTSG

symptom: Application Gateway cannot reach backend; TCP SYN sent but SYN-ACK never received; backend health probe fails
rootCause: NSG on the backend subnet is blocking inbound traffic from AppGW subnet or blocking return path
solution: Review NSG rules on the backend subnet; allow traffic from AppGW subnet IP ranges; correct NSG misconfiguration. Root Cause: Windows Azure\Virtual Network\NSG\Configuration\Customer misconfiguration
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Tools and TSGs/BackendConnectivityTSG

symptom: Application Gateway backend connectivity fails; intermediate firewall drops packets; asymmetric routing observed
rootCause: UDR on backend subnet routes return traffic through a firewall/NVA causing asymmetric routing; the firewall drops packets that do not match an existing session
solution: Fix UDR on backend subnet to ensure symmetric routing; remove or adjust routes that force return traffic through NVA; or configure NVA to allow asymmetric routing. Root Cause: Windows Azure\Virtual Network\UDR\Configuration\How to
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Tools and TSGs/BackendConnectivityTSG

symptom: Application Gateway cannot reach backend in a peered VNet; backend health shows Unhealthy or Unknown
rootCause: VNet peering between AppGW VNet and backend VNet is broken, missing, or not properly configured
solution: Verify VNet peering status in both VNets; recreate or fix the peering; ensure 'Allow gateway transit' and 'Use remote gateways' settings are correct. Root Cause: Windows Azure\Virtual Network\VNet Peering\Configuration\How to
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/Tools and TSGs/BackendConnectivityTSG

symptom: Error when increasing WAF max request body size: 'MaxRequestBodySizeInKb is out of range, it should be within 8 and 128'
rootCause: Application Gateway WAF policy is using OWASP CRS older than 3.2; the new higher limits (up to 2MB request body, 4GB file upload) are only available with CRS 3.2 or later (which triggers WAF engine upgrade)
solution: 1. Upgrade managed rule set to OWASP 3.2 or later: az network application-gateway waf-policy managed-rule rule-set update --type OWASP --version 3.2. 2. Then increase limits via portal Policy Settings or CLI: az network application-gateway waf-policy update --set policySettings.max_request_body_size_in_kb='2000' policySettings.file_upload_limit_in_mb='3500'. Note: upgrading to CRS 3.2 is irreversible via rollback — test with a new policy first.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/WAF Limit Changes

symptom: AppGW WAF GEOIP.txt file contains IP address ranges in integer format (e.g., 3655065088,3655065151,IQ), making it difficult to read or verify which countries/regions are configured for geographic filtering
rootCause: The GEOIP database used by AppGW WAF stores IP ranges as integer values rather than dotted-decimal notation
solution: Use PowerShell script to convert integer IP ranges to dotted-decimal format: $DB = Get-Content 'GEOIP.txt'; foreach ($Data in $DB) { $Start, $End, $Region = $Data -split ',' -replace '^\s*|\s*$'; $StartIP = [IPAddress]$Start; $EndIP = [IPAddress]$End; write-host "$StartIP,$EndIP,$Region" }
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/How To/Convert WAF GeoIPs from Integer format to IP format

symptom: Customer wants to associate a WAF Policy with a specific URI path in Application Gateway per-URI (per path-rule) granularity, but Azure Portal does not show this option
rootCause: Azure Portal does not support per-URI WAF policy association for path-based rules. Only per-site (per-listener) association is available in the Portal
solution: Use Azure CLI to associate WAF policy per URI path rule: az network application-gateway url-path-map rule create -g <RG> --gateway-name <GW-Name> -n <RuleName> --path-map-name <PathMapName> --paths /String/* --address-pool <Pool> --http-settings <Settings> --waf-policy <WAF-Policy-ResourceID>. WAF policy priority: per-URI > per-site > global.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/How To/Create Per Uri Waf Policy for AppGW WAFV2

symptom: Customer needs to migrate Application Gateway from WAF_v2 SKU back to Standard_v2 SKU and receives errors or is unsure how to accomplish the SKU downgrade
rootCause: Migrating from WAF_v2 to Standard_v2 requires removing all WAF policy associations first; there is no direct portal option to change SKU tier
solution: Step 1: Dissociate all per-listener WAF policies and per-URI WAF policies from the AppGW. Step 2: Run PowerShell: $AppGw = Get-AzApplicationGateway -ResourceGroupName <RG> -Name <GWName>; $AppGw.FirewallPolicy = $null; $AppGw.Sku.Name = 'Standard_v2'; $AppGw.Sku.Tier = 'Standard_v2'; $AppGw.WebApplicationFirewallConfiguration = $null; $AppGw.ForceFirewallPolicyAssociation = $true; Set-AzApplicationGateway -ApplicationGateway $AppGw
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/How To/How to use PS to migrate from Waf_v2 to Standard_v2

symptom: Customer requests rollback from CRS 3.2 back to CRS 3.1 or CRS 3.0 on Application Gateway WAF
rootCause: CRS 3.2 introduced a new WAF engine. The new engine does not support rollback to previous engine versions. As of September 2025, rollback is permanently disabled
solution: Do NOT create ICM for rollback (as of Sep 2025 this is no longer permitted). Instruct customer to upgrade directly to the latest DRS 2.1 ruleset. For safe validation of new rules, change the new rules' action to 'log' mode first before enforcing. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/web-application-firewall/ag/application-gateway-crs-rulegroups-rules?tabs=drs21%2Cowasp32#core-rule-sets-crs---legacy
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/How To/Rollback Customer From CRS3.2

symptom: Customer gets error 'MaxRequestBodySizeInKb 2000 is out of range, it should be within 8 and 128' when attempting to increase WAF max request body size beyond 128KB on AppGW WAF_v2
rootCause: The increased request body limit (up to 2MB) and file upload limit (up to 4GB) are only available when running OWASP_3.2 (CRS 3.2) Managed Rule Set. Customer is using an older rule set (OWASP_3.1 or earlier) which has a hard cap of 128KB for request body
solution: Step 1: Upgrade Managed Rule Set to OWASP_3.2 via Portal (WAF Policy → Managed Rules → set to OWASP 3.2 → Save) or CLI: az network application-gateway waf-policy managed-rule rule-set update --policy-name <name> --resource-group <rg> --type OWASP --version 3.2. Step 2: Increase limits: az network application-gateway waf-policy update --name <name> --resource-group <rg> --set policySettings.fileUploadLimitInMb=<MB> policySettings.maxRequestBodySizeInKb=<KB>. Limits: max 2000KB request body, max 4096MB file upload. Note: recommend testing CRS 3.2 on a non-production policy first.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/How To/WAFv2 Increase the Max Request Body size or the Max File Upload Size

symptom: WAF Bot protection blocking an IP address that is not listed as malicious in Interflow / IP appears legitimate but 403 blocked by MicrosoftBotProtection rules
rootCause: Microsoft can insert IPs into the bot/malicious IP list (IPWatchlist) for DDoS or attack activity on Microsoft services in a way that is invisible to the Interflow web portal
solution: 1) Use Interflow Swagger API (https://aka.ms/interflowswagger) → GET /api/ipwatchlist/getsnapshot to download the full bot IP list and verify if the IP appears there. 2) If customer needs to unblock the IP, create a WAF custom rule to Allow traffic from that specific IP — this bypasses both managed rules and bot rules.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/Troubleshooting/HowTo: View complete malicious or non human IP address list

symptom: Application Gateway WAF returning 413 Request Entity Too Large for non-file request bodies even with custom rules applied to skip body inspection
rootCause: The 128 KB body size limit for non-file (raw JSON) requests is enforced by ModSecurity's secrequestbodyinmemorylimit — WAF custom rules skip variable processing but cannot bypass this size limit. Rules and limits are processed independently.
solution: 1) If sending large raw JSON bodies: the 128KB limit is a hard constraint — either reduce body size or disable body inspection for that path. 2) Use per-listener or per-URI WAF policy to disable body inspection only on specific URIs (e.g. /files/upload) while keeping WAF active elsewhere. 3) Upgrade to OWASP 3.2 policy which can increase body size limit to 2MB.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/Troubleshooting/TSG: Application Gateway V2 returning 413 Request Entity Too Large even with custom rules applied

symptom: Application Gateway WAF returning 413 Request Entity Too Large when uploading files larger than 128 KB
rootCause: Request is missing Content-Type and Content-Disposition headers that identify it as a file upload. Without these headers, AppGW treats the body as raw JSON and applies the 128 KB non-file limit instead of the file upload limit (100 MB / 500 MB / 750 MB depending on SKU).
solution: Ensure the file upload request includes: (1) Content-Type header indicating the file MIME type, (2) Content-Disposition header with filename and file attributes. This causes AppGW to treat the request as a file upload and apply the higher file size limit.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/Troubleshooting/TSG: Application Gateway V2 returning 413 Request Entity Too Large even with custom rules applied

symptom: WAF logs show default/managed rules being processed and evaluated even though an incoming request already matched a custom rule
rootCause: By design — WAF was updated to process default rules concurrently with custom rules for efficiency. The logs may show default rules being evaluated, but this does NOT mean the request fate is determined by default rules.
solution: No action required. The request Allow/Block outcome is always determined by the custom rule action when there is a custom rule match. Default rule log entries in this scenario can be safely ignored — they do not affect the final decision.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Application Gateway/WAF for Application Gateway/Troubleshooting/WAF RulesProcessed Even with CustomRuleMatch

symptom: Customer reports security vulnerabilities on Azure Bastion public IP: HTTP OPTIONS method enabled, TLS static key ciphers, untrusted X.509 certificate, weak MAC cipher suites, AngularJS end-of-life library
rootCause: These configurations are by design: (1) HTTP OPTIONS is required for Bastion to work with Azure Portal. (2) TLS 1.2 cipher list follows Microsoft security compliance policy. (3) Certificates are managed and rotated every 90 days. (4) No problematic ciphers per RFC 9113. (5) AngularJS EOL — PG is migrating to a different framework (no ETA).
solution: Inform customer: (1) HTTP OPTIONS cannot be disabled — required for portal functionality. (2) Cipher list is not customer-configurable; maintained per Microsoft security policy. (3) Certificates auto-rotate every 90 days. (4) AngularJS vulnerability: PG is actively working on migration to new framework; no current active vulnerability exploitable. For other unreported vulnerabilities, escalate via TA/AVA to PG and document in the wiki.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/Bastion Vulnerabilities

symptom: Customer wants to change existing regular Azure Bastion deployment to private-only deployment, or cannot configure private-only Bastion
rootCause: Private-only Bastion must be configured at initial deployment time and requires Premium SKU. There is no in-place conversion from regular deployment to private-only.
solution: 1) Delete the existing Bastion deployment from the VNet. 2) Redeploy Bastion with Premium SKU and enable the Private IP address (private-only) option during deployment. The AzureBastionSubnet does not need to be deleted and recreated. Note: Private-only Bastion does not allow public IP access — users must connect via ExpressRoute private-peering to access the bastion private IP.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/Features/Feature: Bastion Private-Only Deployment

symptom: Microsoft Entra ID login option not displayed as an authentication option in Azure Bastion for Linux VMs
rootCause: AADSSHLogin/AADSSHLoginForLinux extension not installed on VM, or required RBAC role (Virtual Machine Administrator Login / Virtual Machine User Login) not assigned, or system-assigned managed identity not enabled on VM
solution: 1. Verify AADSSHLogin or AADSSHLoginForLinux extension is listed under VM > Extensions + Applications; 2. Assign 'Virtual Machine Administrator Login' or 'Virtual Machine User Login' role to user on VM; 3. Ensure system-assigned managed identity is enabled on VM; 4. If all correct, check portal telemetry via Kusto: cluster('azportalpartner').database('AzurePortal').ExtEvents filtering on extension='Microsoft_Azure_HybridNetworking' and area='BastionHost.getVmExtensionsForVmId' or 'BastionHostAjax.getRoleAssignmentsForVmAssignedToPrincipal'
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/Features/Feature: Bastion SSH + EntraID authentication through Portal

symptom: Portal shows error 'There was an error in fetching ssh cert token for Entra ID authentication. Please try again.' when connecting via Bastion Entra ID login
rootCause: Problem communicating with Bastion for keys or fetching token from portal side; may involve failures in cert token fetch or RSA key retrieval
solution: Check portal telemetry using Kusto: cluster('azportalpartner').database('AzurePortal').ExtEvents filtering on extension='Microsoft_Azure_HybridNetworking', area='BastionLoginWindowV2._fetchSshCertTokenAndConnect' or 'BastionLoginWindowV2._getRsaKeys' to identify specific failure
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/Features/Feature: Bastion SSH + EntraID authentication through Portal

symptom: Entra ID login option displayed in Bastion but authentication fails; or option appears but login does not work for certain Linux VM
rootCause: Linux distribution or version does not support Entra ID (AAD) login. Some unsupported OS versions allow extension installation post-deployment but the OS itself cannot authenticate via AAD.
solution: Verify Linux distro is in supported list: Common Base Linux Mariner, CentOS, Debian, openSUSE, RHEL, SLES, Ubuntu Server. Unsupported distros cannot authenticate even with extension installed. Ref: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/devices/howto-vm-sign-in-azure-ad-linux
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/Features/Feature: Bastion SSH + EntraID authentication through Portal

symptom: Bastion session recording connections fail after enabling Session Recording feature; sessions cannot be established or recordings not saved
rootCause: No valid SAS URL provided after enabling session recording, or SAS URL has expired. Bastion requires a valid SAS URL pointing to a storage account container to store recordings.
solution: Generate a new SAS token from the storage container: 1. Right-click container > Generate SAS; 2. Permissions: READ, ADD, CREATE, WRITE, LIST; 3. Set start time at least 15 minutes before current time (clock skew prevention); 4. Copy Blob SAS URL; 5. In Bastion > Session Recording blade > Add or update SAS URL. Note: use access policy without expiration OR track expiry manually.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/Features/Feature: Bastion Session Recording

symptom: Bastion session recording fails; Bastion unable to push recordings to storage account even with valid SAS URL
rootCause: CORS policy not correctly configured on storage account, preventing Bastion from writing to the storage container
solution: Go to Storage Account > Resource Sharing (CORS) > Blob service. Add/update CORS policy: set Allowed Origins to either '*' or Bastion DNS 'https://bst-<armguid>.bastion.azure.com'; set Allowed Methods to at least DELETE, GET. Note: updating storage account or container while connections are active will cause connection failures.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/Features/Feature: Bastion Session Recording

symptom: 'Reset Password' option not visible when connecting via Azure Bastion Shareable Link, even when VM local account has 'User must change password at Next Logon' configured
rootCause: By design: Bastion Shareable Link is intended for external/third-party users. Exposing password reset to third parties would be a security risk. Reset Password is only available from Azure Portal Bastion blade, not via Shareable Link.
solution: Inform customer this is expected behavior by design. To reset password, use the Azure Portal Bastion blade directly (not the Shareable Link flow). Standard SKU required for Shareable Link.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/Features/Feature: Bastion Shareable Link

symptom: Azure Bastion expected to use Kerberos authentication but silently falls back to NTLM; Tomcat log shows 'fallback to non-Kerberos IpResolver'
rootCause: Kerberos authentication setup incomplete: Bastion environment missing required Active Directory Domain Services (must include Kerberos, LDAP, DNS, and time services), or Kerberos not enabled in Bastion Configuration blade
solution: 1. Enable Kerberos authentication in Bastion Configuration blade; 2. Verify Bastion environment has its own ADDS providing Kerberos+LDAP+DNS+time services; 3. Validate via Tomcat logs: success = 'Resolving ... with Kerberos enabled', failure = 'fallback to non-Kerberos IpResolver {}'; 4. ASC will show 'Kerberos Enabled: true/false' under Bastion Properties post-GA; 5. Use Vulcan for portal validation
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/Features/Feature: Kerberos Support for Azure Bastion

symptom: Customer gets black screen when trying to RDP or SSH to Azure VM via Bastion; ASC Diagnostics shows connectivity status 'Failed'
rootCause: User-defined routes (UDR) on VM subnet causing asymmetric routing — traffic is routed through an NVA instead of returning directly to Bastion
solution: 1. Check UDRs on both VM subnet and Bastion subnet via ASC Diagnostics > Azure Bastion Connectivity Checker; 2. Add a more specific route to the Bastion subnet CIDR (e.g., 10.169.245.0/27) with next hop 'Virtual Network' instead of NVA; 3. Validate fix by re-running ASC connectivity check — status should change to 'Ok'
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/How To/Bastion Unable to RDP or SSH Results in Black Screen

symptom: Bastion RDP/SSH results in black screen or error 'Target Machine taking too long' even after verifying network connectivity is OK
rootCause: WebSockets traffic blocked by customer's firewall or web proxy; Bastion connection requires WebSocket upgrade (wss://) which may be dropped silently
solution: 1. Gather browser HAR trace while customer attempts connection; 2. Open HAR in Fiddler or browser DevTools; 3. Look for WebSocket upgrade calls (wss:// URLs) — expected: client sends HTTP upgrade request, server responds HTTP 101 with 'Connection: upgrade'; 4. If no server response (HTTP 0 in Fiddler) or HTTP errors on WebSocket calls, ask customer security team to allow WebSockets traffic; Ref: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/bastion/troubleshoot#blackscreen
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/How To/Bastion Unable to RDP or SSH Results in Black Screen

symptom: 启用 JIT Access 后 Azure Bastion 无法连接目标 VM，NSG 中 JIT Deny Rule（Priority 4096）阻断了 RDP/SSH 端口
rootCause: Microsoft Defender for Cloud JIT 策略在 VM NSG 上创建 Priority 4096 的 Deny Rule，当没有更高优先级 Allow Rule 允许 AzureBastionSubnet 流量时，Bastion 连接被拒绝
solution: 在 VM NIC/Subnet 的 NSG 中为 AzureBastionSubnet CIDR 创建更高优先级的 Allow Rule（inbound 允许 3389/22），使其优先于 JIT Deny Rule 生效；即使 JIT Access 过期，高优先级规则仍保障 Bastion 连接。若需限制为 JIT 方式访问，可通过 MDC → JIT VM Access → Edit Port Configuration 将 AzureBastionSubnet CIDR 添加到允许范围
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/How To/JIT access with Bastion

symptom: 通过 Azure Bastion 连接时身份验证失败，启用 MCAS/Conditional Access 策略的外部用户无法登录 Bastion
rootCause: Bastion 内置安全机制仅允许来自 portal.azure.com 域的访问；启用 MCAS 或 Conditional Access 后访问域被重定向到其他 URL（如 cas.ms/mcas.ms），导致 Bastion 域验证失败
solution: 当前 MCAS（Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps）Conditional Access 不被 Bastion 支持（Feature Request Task 10883555 已提交 PG）。短期缓解：禁用对 Bastion 访问的 Conditional Access 策略，或排除 portal.azure.com 的 Bastion 访问路径不受 MCAS 拦截；长期需等待 PG 实现该功能
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/How To/MCAS is Not Supported on Bastion

symptom: 通过 Azure Bastion 连接 VM 需要多次尝试才能成功，第一次连接经常超时或失败
rootCause: 客户端浏览器或代理阻断了 WebSocket（wss://）连接；Bastion 优先使用 WebSocket，失败后回落到 HTTP Tunnel（效率低，延迟高），HAR 日志中可见 WebSocket 连接失败后切换到 HTTP tunnel
solution: 1. 浏览器 Console 执行 window.WebSocket 确认支持；2. 访问 https://groklearning.com/debug/blocked/ 检测 WebSocket 是否被阻断；3. 检查 Windows 代理设置（UI 或 CMD）；4. 在代理白名单中添加 Bastion DNS（Portal Overview 或 ASC 可见）；或配置 Bastion 流量绕过代理
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/How To/Troubleshoot Multiple Attempts To Connect vm via Bastion issue

symptom: Azure Bastion Session Recording 配置后连接报错：Cannot Connect to session without valid SAS URL. Disable session recording on the bastion or update bastion with valid SAS URL and try reconnecting
rootCause: 1. SAS Token 已过期；2. Storage Account 防火墙配置为 Selected Virtual Networks 但未包含 AzureBastionSubnet，导致 Bastion 无法挂载存储（Tomcat 日志/HTTP 500 可见 mount is down）
solution: 1. 重新生成 SAS Token 并更新 Bastion 配置；2. 检查 Storage Account 防火墙 → 将 AzureBastionSubnet 和 VM Subnet 加入 Selected Virtual Networks 允许列表；注意：Session Recording 不支持 Storage Account Private Endpoint
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/How To/Troubleshoot Session Recording

symptom: 通过 Azure Bastion Native Client 无法复制粘贴文本到目标 VM（粘贴按钮置灰或无效）
rootCause: 本地或 Active Directory Group Policy 启用了 'Do not allow clipboard redirection'，阻止 RDP 会话中的剪贴板重定向
solution: 在目标 VM 执行：gpedit.msc → Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Remote Desktop Services > Remote Desktop Session Hosts > Device and Resource Redirection > Do not allow clipboard redirection → Disable；执行 gpupdate /force 后完全注销并重新登录生效（仅断开 RDP 不够）。前提：Bastion Standard SKU + Portal 中已启用 Copy and paste 及 Native client support
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/How To/Unable to Copy Paste Text or File

symptom: 通过 Azure Bastion Native Client 无法传输文件（File Transfer 失败，驱动器无法访问）
rootCause: 本地或 Active Directory Group Policy 启用了 'Do not allow drive redirection'，阻止 RDP 会话中的驱动器重定向（文件传输依赖此功能）
solution: 在目标 VM 执行：gpedit.msc → Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Remote Desktop Services > Remote Desktop Session Hosts > Device and Resource Redirection > Do not allow drive redirection → Disable；执行 gpupdate /force 后完全注销并重新登录生效。注意：修改 Domain Controller 的 Group Policy 影响范围广，需谨慎
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/How To/Unable to Copy Paste Text or File

symptom: 无法通过 Azure Bastion 连接 VM（Portal 报 'target machine unreachable or credentials incorrect'），Daemon 日志显示 'license connection sequence aborted' / 'ERRCONNECT_CONNECT_TRANSPORT_FAILED [0x0002000D]'，直连 VM 显示 Error Code 0x808 Extended 0x101
rootCause: VM 上安装了 Remote Desktop Session Host (RDSH) Role 但 RDS Grace Period 已过期或未配置 RD Licensing Server（Windows Event ID 50280: RD Licensing grace period expired；Event ID 1068: Licensing mode not configured）
solution: 无 RDS Farm 需求：通过 Server Manager → Remove Roles → Remote Desktop Session Host 卸载角色；或通过 Serial Console 执行 powershell: Uninstall-WindowsFeature -Name RDS-RD-Server -Restart。有 RDS Farm：需配置有效 RD Licensing Server 并购买 CAL 许可证。可用 ASC Inspect IaaS Disk / WinGuest Analyzer 确认 RDP Event 日志；注意移除 RDS 后最多允许 2 个并发连接
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/How To/Unable to use Bastion to connect to a VM due to RDS licensing issue

symptom: Azure Bastion 部署失败，错误：Public IP Allocation failed. Please increase Public IP quota and try again. (Code: GatewayAllocationFailedDueToInsuffcientPublicIPs)
rootCause: 订阅的 Public IP 配额耗尽，涉及多种配额类型：maxPublicIpsPerSubscription、maxStaticPublicIpsPerSubscription、maxStandardSkuPublicIpsPerSubscription 之一超限
solution: 通过 Jarvis → NRP Quota Operation → Get NRP Subscription Quota Details 检查订阅当前各类 Public IP 配额用量；确认超限的配额类型后，开 Collaboration 给 Subscription Team 申请提升对应配额
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/How To/Deployment Issues

symptom: Azure Bastion Kerberos authentication fails after DNS server changes on the VNet; users cannot authenticate using Kerberos
rootCause: Changes to DNS server are not supported for Kerberos authentication in Azure Bastion. The Bastion service does not pick up DNS changes dynamically.
solution: Delete and re-create the Azure Bastion resource after making any DNS server changes to the VNet.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureNetworking/Wiki:/Azure Bastion/How To/Verify Bastion is using Kerberos
